statement: Eyes with resolving power have evolved in ten fundamentally different forms across the animal kingdom.
quizQuestion: How many fundamentally different eye forms have evolved across animals?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, eyes with resolving power have come in ten fundamentally different forms, classified broadly as compound eyes and non-compound eyes. Compound eyes (insects, crustaceans) use multiple small visual units, while non-compound eyes (mammals) use a single lens focusing onto the retina.
wowFactor: Evolution independently invented the eye at least ten different ways — nature kept trying until it got vision right!

variants.question: How many fundamentally different eye forms have evolved across animals?
True or false: compound eyes are common in mammals, while non-compound eyes appear in insects.
A dragonfly's eye is made of many small visual units. What type of eye is this?
The eye connects to the visual cortex via the ___ nerve.

visualDescription: A mosaic of glowing eyes in the dark — dragonfly compound facets, cat slit pupils, human irises, and deep-sea orbs all arranged like stained glass, each a different evolutionary invention.

statement: The simplest eyes in nature are pit eyes — shallow depressions that let organisms detect the angle of incoming light.
quizQuestion: What are the simplest type of eyes in the animal kingdom called?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the simplest eyes are pit eyes — eye-spots set into a pit that reduces the angle of light entering, allowing the organism to detect the direction of a light source. These are far more primitive than the lens-based eyes of vertebrates or the compound eyes of insects.
wowFactor: Before eyes had lenses or retinas, they were just tiny pits in skin — ancient creatures could 'see' light direction with what's basically a dimple!

variants.question: What animal feature reduces light angle to help detect its direction without a lens?
True or false: pit eyes are the most complex type of eye found in the animal kingdom.
The simplest eyes are called ___ eyes, and they can only detect light direction.
An early sea creature can detect which side light comes from but cannot form images. What eye type does it likely have?

visualDescription: A primitive flatworm in a dark ocean, its body surface dotted with tiny pigment pits glowing faintly in bioluminescence — nature's first crude attempt at seeing.

statement: The eye controls circadian rhythms by sending light signals directly to the brain's internal clock via the retinohypothalamic tract.
quizQuestion: Which eye-to-brain pathway regulates your body's daily internal clock?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, retinal photosensitive ganglion cells send signals along the retinohypothalamic tract to the suprachiasmatic nuclei to effect circadian adjustment. This means eyes do far more than just see — they literally set your body clock every day.
wowFactor: Your eyes aren't just cameras — they're also clocks. Special cells in your retina send direct signals to reset your brain's 24-hour timer every morning!

variants.question: True or false: the eye performs photo-response functions completely independent of vision.
A blind person's retinal ganglion cells still send light signals to the suprachiasmatic nuclei. What function does this serve?
The retinohypothalamic tract connects the eye to the ___ nuclei to regulate the body clock.
Which organ sends light signals that reset the brain's circadian pacemaker each day?

visualDescription: A glowing retina cross-section with a golden signal beam traveling from a photosensitive ganglion cell through a luminous tract to a small clock-like nucleus deep in the brain.

statement: The human cerebral cortex contains approximately 14 to 16 billion neurons.
quizQuestion: Roughly how many neurons are in the human cerebral cortex?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the human cerebral cortex contains approximately 14–16 billion neurons. The cerebral cortex is the outermost layer of the brain responsible for thought, consciousness, and higher functions — making it the densest known concentration of cognitive processing in the known universe.
wowFactor: Your brain's outer layer alone holds 14–16 billion neurons — more than twice the human population of Earth, all crammed into something the size of a napkin folded up.

variants.question: Roughly how many neurons are in the human cerebral cortex?
True or false: the human cerebral cortex contains more neurons than there are people on Earth.
Which part of the brain contains the majority of neurons responsible for thought and consciousness?
The brain and spinal cord together form the ___ nervous system.

visualDescription: A glowing human brain cross-section lit in electric blue, billions of tiny sparking neurons visible in the folded cortex surface like a galaxy of stars compressed into a tight spiral.

statement: All vertebrate brains can be embryonically divided into three parts: the forebrain, midbrain, and hindbrain.
quizQuestion: How many embryonic parts is every vertebrate brain divided into?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, all vertebrate brains develop embryonically from the midline dorsal nerve cord and can be divided into forebrain (prosencephalon), midbrain (mesencephalon), and hindbrain (rhombencephalon). This ancient three-part plan is shared across fish, frogs, mice, and humans.
wowFactor: From fish to humans, every vertebrate brain starts as the same three-part blueprint — we share the same basic brain architecture with animals that lived 500 million years ago.

variants.question: How many embryonic parts is every vertebrate brain divided into?
Which three-part developmental structure is shared by all vertebrate brains?
True or false: human brains and fish brains share the same basic three-part embryonic structure.
The hindbrain's full scientific name is the ___.

visualDescription: Side-by-side pixel art embryos of a fish, frog, and human, each with an identical glowing three-segment brain structure highlighted in gold, teal, and purple.

statement: The concentration of the brain in the head — a pattern called cephalization — evolved because the head is where most sensory organs are located.
quizQuestion: What is the biological term for the evolutionary concentration of the brain in the head?
explanation: According to Wikidata, the brain's location in the head is caused by cephalization — the evolutionary trend of concentrating nervous tissue and sense organs at the front of the body. Since most sensory organs (vision, hearing, smell) are near the mouth, placing the brain close to them speeds up response time.
wowFactor: Your brain is in your head for a surprisingly practical reason — it's next to your eyes, ears, and nose so nerve signals don't have to travel as far to be processed!

variants.question: Which evolutionary trend places the brain near the sensory organs in the head?
True or false: invertebrate brains typically arise from paired ganglia rather than a single centralized structure.
The process of the brain becoming concentrated in the head during evolution is called ___.
A worm's nerve clusters control only their own body segment. What kind of nervous organization does this represent?

visualDescription: An evolutionary timeline showing a flatworm with scattered nerve dots, a fish with a small head-cluster, and a human skull with a bright glowing brain — cephalization in three steps.

statement: Nervous tissue first arose in wormlike organisms approximately 550 to 600 million years ago.
quizQuestion: How many million years ago did nervous tissue first appear in animals?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, nervous tissue first arose in wormlike organisms about 550 to 600 million years ago. This means the fundamental architecture of our nervous system has roots that predate the dinosaurs by over 300 million years.
wowFactor: The basic wiring of your nervous system is 550 million years old — your neurons are following a blueprint invented by ancient sea worms long before fish, dinosaurs, or mammals existed!

variants.question: How many million years ago did nervous tissue first appear in animals?
True or false: nervous tissue in animals first appeared before the dinosaurs evolved.
A paleontologist finds a 570-million-year-old worm fossil with nerve tissue. Is this consistent with the known timeline of nervous system evolution?
distractors: Only if it has a brain

variants.question: In vertebrates, the nervous system consists of two parts: the ___ nervous system and the peripheral nervous system.

visualDescription: An ancient Precambrian seafloor with glowing worm-like creatures trailing faint bioluminescent nerve threads, while a timeline bar stretches back 550 million years into the dark water.

statement: The enteric nervous system is a dedicated branch of the autonomic nervous system that independently controls the entire gastrointestinal tract.
quizQuestion: Which branch of the nervous system controls the gastrointestinal tract?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the enteric nervous system is a subdivision of the autonomic nervous system that functions to control the gastrointestinal system. It is sometimes called the 'second brain' because it can operate independently of the central nervous system.
wowFactor: Your gut has its own dedicated nervous system with millions of neurons that can operate completely independently from your brain — that's why people call it the 'second brain'!

variants.question: What is the nickname for the enteric nervous system, which controls digestion independently?
True or false: the sympathetic nervous system activates during emergencies to mobilize energy.
The autonomic nervous system is subdivided into the sympathetic, parasympathetic, and ___ systems.
You feel your stomach 'clench' during stress. Which part of the autonomic nervous system is responsible?

visualDescription: A cross-section of human intestine glowing with a dense web of golden nerve fibers threaded throughout the gut wall, completely separate from the brain illustration above.

statement: Nerves sending signals from the brain to the body are called motor (efferent) nerves, while nerves carrying signals back to the brain are called sensory (afferent) nerves.
quizQuestion: What are nerves that carry signals FROM the brain to the body called?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, nerves that transmit signals from the brain are called motor nerves (efferent), while those that transmit information from the body to the brain are called sensory nerves (afferent). 'Efferent' comes from Latin for 'carrying away' and 'afferent' from 'carrying toward.'
wowFactor: Every nerve in your body is either a one-way 'outgoing' or 'incoming' line — motor nerves shout orders from the brain, sensory nerves whisper reports back.

variants.question: What term describes nerves that carry signals FROM the body TO the brain?
True or false: efferent nerves carry sensory information toward the brain.
Nerves exiting from the spinal cord are called spinal nerves; those exiting from the brain are called ___ nerves.
Your brain sends a command to your hand to grip a ball. Which type of nerve carries this signal?

visualDescription: A human silhouette with two colored nerve pathways — blue arrows flowing from brain down to fingers (motor/efferent) and red arrows flowing from fingertips back up to brain (sensory/afferent).

statement: Alzheimer's disease is the most common form of dementia, accounting for around 60 to 70 percent of all cases.
quizQuestion: What percentage of dementia cases does Alzheimer's disease account for?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Alzheimer's disease accounts for around 60–70% of all dementia cases, making it by far the most common neurodegenerative disease. The earliest and most common symptom is difficulty remembering recent events.
wowFactor: Two out of every three people with dementia have Alzheimer's — it's not just a type of dementia, it practically IS dementia.

variants.question: What percentage of dementia cases does Alzheimer's disease account for?
True or false: the most common early symptom of Alzheimer's disease is difficulty remembering recent events.
What is the average life expectancy following an Alzheimer's disease diagnosis?
Alzheimer's disease is characterized by accumulation of amyloid plaques and neurofibrillary ___ in the brain.

statement: The strongest genetic risk factor for Alzheimer's disease is a variant of the apolipoprotein E gene, which is involved in fat metabolism.
quizQuestion: Which protein's gene variant is the strongest known genetic risk factor for Alzheimer's?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the strongest genetic risk factor for Alzheimer's is from an allele of apolipoprotein E, a protein involved in the metabolism of fats in mammals. Having certain variants of this gene significantly increases lifetime risk of developing the disease.
wowFactor: The biggest genetic risk factor for memory-destroying Alzheimer's isn't a brain gene — it's a fat-processing gene, revealing a surprising link between cholesterol metabolism and dementia.

variants.question: True or false: apolipoprotein E is involved in fat metabolism in mammals.

statement: After an Alzheimer's disease diagnosis, average life expectancy is three to twelve years.
quizQuestion: What is the average life expectancy range after an Alzheimer's diagnosis?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, although the speed of progression can vary, the average life expectancy following an Alzheimer's diagnosis is three to twelve years. The wide range reflects that some patients decline rapidly while others progress slowly over a decade.
wowFactor: Alzheimer's progression varies so wildly that someone diagnosed today might have just 3 years or a full 12 — doctors genuinely can't predict which path each patient will take.

variants.question: What is the average life expectancy range after an Alzheimer's diagnosis?
True or false: Alzheimer's disease always progresses at the same pace for all patients.
Which treatment listed in Wikidata is used for Alzheimer's disease?
As Alzheimer's advances, people gradually ___ from family and society.

sensitivityNote: Disease prognosis topic — handled factually and without distress framing.

statement: There are two main types of stroke: ischemic (from lack of blood flow) and hemorrhagic (from bleeding).
quizQuestion: What are the two main types of stroke?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, there are two main types of stroke: ischemic, due to lack of blood flow, and hemorrhagic, due to bleeding. Both cause parts of the brain to stop functioning properly, but they require different treatments — which is why brain imaging is critical for diagnosis.
wowFactor: The two types of stroke are nearly opposite medical emergencies — one from too little blood and one from too much — and treating one the wrong way can make the other fatal!

variants.question: True or false: a transient ischemic attack (TIA) has symptoms lasting less than 24 hours.

visualDescription: Split pixel art brain cross-section: left half shows a dark blocked artery with dead gray tissue (ischemic), right half shows a burst vessel with red bleeding spreading through tissue (hemorrhagic).

statement: High blood pressure is the single most significant risk factor for stroke.
quizQuestion: What is the most significant risk factor for stroke?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the most significant risk factor for stroke is high blood pressure. Other risk factors include high cholesterol, tobacco smoking, obesity, diabetes, previous TIA, kidney disease, and atrial fibrillation — but hypertension tops the list.
wowFactor: High blood pressure is the number-one cause of stroke — a silent condition with no symptoms that's quietly responsible for millions of brain injuries every year.

variants.question: True or false: high blood pressure is the most significant risk factor for stroke.
A doctor wants to reduce a patient's stroke risk. Which condition should they prioritize treating above all others?
A stroke where symptoms last less than 24 hours is called a ___ ischemic attack.

statement: Stroke has an incidence rate of 213 cases per 100,000 person-years.
quizQuestion: How many stroke cases occur per 100,000 person-years worldwide?
explanation: According to Wikidata, stroke has an incidence rate of 213 cases per 100,000 person-years. This makes it one of the leading medical emergencies globally, affecting people across every demographic.
wowFactor: Every 100,000 people produce 213 strokes per year — meaning in a city of a million, over 2,000 strokes happen annually, or roughly 6 every single day.

variants.question: How many stroke cases occur per 100,000 person-years worldwide?

statement: The two most common treatment options for mental disorders are psychotherapy and psychiatric medication.
quizQuestion: What are the two most common treatments for mental disorders?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, common treatment options for mental disorders are psychotherapy or psychiatric medication, with lifestyle changes and social interventions also playing a role. Wikidata confirms antidepressants, mood stabilizers, anxiolytics, and antipsychotics among specific medication types used.
wowFactor: Mental disorders — from anxiety to schizophrenia — are treated with the same basic two-tool kit as they were 50 years ago: talk therapy and medications, combined in different ways.

statement: A mental disorder is clinically defined as a behavioral or mental pattern causing significant distress or impairment of personal functioning.
quizQuestion: What core criterion defines a mental disorder clinically?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, a mental disorder is characterized by a clinically significant disturbance in cognition, emotional regulation, or behavior that causes significant distress or impairment of personal functioning. Unusual behavior alone is not enough — it must meaningfully impact the person's life.
wowFactor: By clinical definition, unusual or eccentric behavior alone doesn't make something a mental disorder — it only counts if it genuinely disrupts someone's ability to function in daily life.

variants.question: True or false: unusual behavior alone qualifies as a mental disorder if others find it strange.

statement: The World Health Organization estimates that about 1 in 100 children were diagnosed with autism between 2012 and 2021.
quizQuestion: According to the WHO, roughly how many children are diagnosed with autism?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the World Health Organization estimates that about 1 in 100 children were diagnosed between 2012 and 2021, noting an increasing trend. This rise is largely attributed to broader diagnostic criteria and greater awareness rather than a true increase in prevalence.
wowFactor: One in every 100 kids is diagnosed with autism — and since adults rarely get formally assessed, the real number across all ages may be far higher than official figures suggest.

variants.question: According to the WHO, roughly how many children are diagnosed with autism?

statement: Autism is highly heritable and involves many genes, while environmental factors play a smaller, mainly prenatal role.
quizQuestion: What is the primary driver of autism risk: genetics or environment?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, autism is highly heritable and involves many genes, while environmental factors appear to play a smaller, mainly prenatal role. This directly contradicts the long-debunked claim that vaccines cause autism — the evidence firmly points to genetics as the dominant factor.
wowFactor: Autism is one of the most heritable conditions in psychiatry — genetics plays a larger role than in most mental health conditions, and the 'vaccines cause autism' myth was debunked long ago.

variants.question: True or false: environmental factors play a larger role than genetics in causing autism.

statement: Boys are diagnosed with autism several times more often than girls, partly because girls are better at hiding autistic traits.
quizQuestion: Why are boys diagnosed with autism more often than girls, according to research?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, boys are diagnosed several times more often than girls, with girls being better at hiding autistic traits — a phenomenon sometimes called 'masking' or 'camouflaging'. This means many autistic girls go undiagnosed for years, often receiving an incorrect diagnosis first.
wowFactor: Autism is diagnosed far more in boys than girls — not necessarily because boys have it more, but because girls are better at 'masking' their symptoms, leaving many undiagnosed for years!

variants.question: True or false: girls with autism tend to hide their autistic traits better than boys do.

statement: The DSM-5, the main mental disorder diagnostic manual, was criticized at launch because it lacks objective biomarkers — unlike nearly every other medical specialty.
quizQuestion: What key diagnostic tool does the DSM-5 lack compared to other medical specialties?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, when DSM-5 was published in May 2013, the then-director of NIMH noted it lacked validity compared to other medical specialties because it has no objective laboratory measures (biomarkers) for diagnosis. All other major medical specialties rely on measurable biological markers.
wowFactor: The bible of mental health diagnosis has no blood test, no brain scan, no biomarker — psychiatry is one of the only medical fields that cannot measure what it treats!

variants.question: True or false: the DSM-5 was published in May 2013.

statement: Mental disorders are diagnosed using two main manuals: the ICD (edited by the WHO) and the DSM (published by the American Psychiatric Association).
quizQuestion: Which UN body edits the ICD manual for diagnosing mental disorders?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, mental disorders are diagnosed using the International Classification of Diseases (ICD), edited by the World Health Organization, and the DSM published by the American Psychiatric Association. The two manuals serve different parts of the global medical community.
wowFactor: The world uses two competing bibles to define mental illness — one from the WHO and one from America, and they do not always agree on what counts as a disorder.

statement: Most psychologists believe intelligence is not a single unified capacity but can be divided into various domains or competencies.
quizQuestion: What do most psychologists believe about how intelligence is structured?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, most psychologists believe intelligence can be divided into various domains or competencies rather than being a single unified capacity. Intelligence has been measured using tools like the Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale and Raven's Progressive Matrices, each targeting different aspects.
wowFactor: There is no single intelligence score hiding in your DNA — most scientists believe intelligence is a mosaic of separate abilities, not one thing you simply have more or less of.

variants.question: True or false: most psychologists view intelligence as a single unified capacity.

statement: Some researchers suggest plants may exhibit forms of intelligence, though this remains highly controversial in science.
quizQuestion: Which surprising organisms have researchers controversially proposed may show intelligence?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, while intelligence has been studied extensively in humans and non-human animals, some researchers have suggested that plants may exhibit forms of intelligence — though this claim remains controversial and is not widely accepted by mainstream science.
wowFactor: Some scientists think plants might be intelligent — quietly processing information and responding to their world, just without a brain!

variants.question: True or false: plant intelligence is widely accepted by mainstream science.

statement: The ability to generate electric signals in nerve cells first appeared in evolution approximately 700 to 800 million years ago, during the Tonian period.
quizQuestion: How far back did the ability to generate nerve electric signals first appear?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, molecular evidence suggests that the ability to generate electric signals (action potentials) first appeared during the Tonian period, roughly 700 to 800 million years ago. Predecessors of neurons were peptidergic secretory cells that eventually gained gene modules enabling fast electrical signals.
wowFactor: The spark of thought — the first nerve electric signal — fired in an ancient sea creature 700 million years before us, and every brain since runs the same basic trick!

variants.question: True or false: plants and fungi have nerve cells like animals do.

statement: Neurons are the main components of nervous tissue in all animals except sponges and placozoans — those two groups have no nerve cells at all.
quizQuestion: Which two animal groups are the only ones with no neurons at all?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, neurons are the main components of nervous tissue in all animals except sponges and placozoans. These two ancient animal groups have no nerve cells whatsoever — making them unique outliers in the animal kingdom.
wowFactor: Sponges are so ancient they skipped getting a nervous system entirely — no neurons, no nerve signals, yet they have survived 600 million years without a single thought!

variants.question: True or false: sponges have neurons like most other animals.

statement: Neurons are classified into three functional types: sensory neurons detect stimuli, motor neurons control movement, and interneurons connect them within the nervous system.
quizQuestion: Into how many functional categories are neurons classified?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, neurons are typically classified into three types based on function. Sensory neurons respond to stimuli such as touch, sound, or light and send signals toward the brain. Motor neurons receive signals from the brain and spinal cord to control everything from muscle movement to organ function.
wowFactor: Every thought, movement, and sensation runs through just three types of nerve cell — the brain's entire repertoire built from three simple building blocks.

variants.question: Which neuron type responds to stimuli like touch or light?

statement: A rash that does NOT fade when a glass is rolled over it is a warning sign of meningitis — a potentially life-threatening brain infection.
quizQuestion: What simple household object can reveal a meningitis warning rash?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, meningitis can cause a non-blanching rash — one that does not fade when a glass is rolled over it. This is a critical emergency warning sign, since meningitis is classified as a medical emergency due to its proximity to the brain and spinal cord.
wowFactor: A drinking glass can be a life-saving medical test — press it on a meningitis rash and it will not fade, screaming emergency to anyone who knows the sign.

variants.question: True or false: a rash that fades under glass pressure is a warning sign of meningitis.

statement: Meningitis is diagnosed by a lumbar puncture — a needle inserted into the spinal canal to collect cerebrospinal fluid for testing.
quizQuestion: Which procedure diagnoses meningitis by sampling spinal canal fluid?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, a lumbar puncture — in which a needle is inserted into the spinal canal to collect cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) — can diagnose or exclude meningitis. Meningitis is classified as a medical emergency because inflammation near the brain and spinal cord can be life-threatening.
wowFactor: To confirm meningitis, doctors literally tap your spine with a needle — one of medicine's most dramatic yet essential diagnostic procedures.

variants.question: True or false: meningitis is classified as a medical emergency.

statement: Some forms of meningitis are preventable by vaccination, including meningococcal, mumps, pneumococcal, and Hib vaccines — four distinct vaccine types.
quizQuestion: How many immunization options exist to prevent certain forms of meningitis?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, some forms of meningitis are preventable by immunization with meningococcal, mumps, pneumococcal, and Hib vaccines — that is four distinct vaccine types. Early antibiotic treatment is also a key intervention in acute meningitis cases.
wowFactor: Four different vaccines guard against meningitis — one of medicine's most powerful prevention success stories for a condition that can kill within hours.

variants.question: True or false: no vaccines currently exist to prevent any form of meningitis.

statement: Alzheimer's disease is the most common form of dementia, which is an incurable, progressive neurocognitive disorder distinct from normal aging.
quizQuestion: Which condition is responsible for the majority of dementia cases?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the most common form of dementia is Alzheimer's disease. Dementia is an incurable, progressive neurocognitive disorder — and critically, it is NOT the same as normal age-related cognitive decline, with no change in intelligence.
wowFactor: Dementia is not just getting old — it is a disease process, and Alzheimer's is the most common culprit, yet it still has no cure.

statement: Dementia is not the same as normal aging — it causes a genuine decline in cognitive abilities, while normal aging does not change intelligence.
quizQuestion: Unlike normal aging, what does a progressive neurocognitive disorder actually affect?
acceptableAnswers: dementia is a disease not aging
explanation: According to Wikipedia, dementia is NOT the same as age-related decline in cognition and memory. Normal aging produces no change in intelligence, whereas dementia is a true progressive neurocognitive disorder that disrupts cognitive abilities and everyday functioning.
wowFactor: Normal aging does not actually make you less intelligent — forgetting where you put your keys is aging; forgetting what keys are for is dementia.

statement: Every sensory receptor has a 'receptive field' — the specific region of the body or environment it responds to, like a single cone in your eye detecting only one tiny patch of light.
quizQuestion: What term describes the specific zone a sensory receptor responds to?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the receptive field is the area of the body or environment to which a receptor organ and receptor cells respond. For instance, the part of the world an eye can see is its receptive field, and the light that each rod or cone can detect is its individual receptive field — a concept identified across visual, auditory, and somatosensory systems.
wowFactor: Each tiny cone in your eye only sees a minuscule patch of the world — your brain then stitches millions of these receptive fields into one seamless image!

variants.question: True or false: the receptive field of an eye is the entire portion of the world it can see.

statement: Humans have more than five senses — commonly recognized sensory systems include vision, hearing, touch, taste, smell, balance, and visceral sensation.
quizQuestion: How many commonly recognized human senses does modern science identify?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, commonly recognized sensory systems include vision, hearing, touch, taste, smell, balance, and visceral sensation — that is at least seven distinct systems, not the classic five taught in school. The sensory nervous system processes all of these through dedicated neural pathways.
wowFactor: You were taught you have 5 senses — but science counts at least 7, including balance and the ability to feel what is happening inside your own body!

statement: Neurology is a nonsurgical medical specialty — its corresponding surgical counterpart is neurosurgery, making them two distinct specialties for the same organ system.
quizQuestion: What is the surgical counterpart to the nonsurgical specialty of neurology?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, neurology is a nonsurgical specialty — its corresponding surgical specialty is neurosurgery. Neurologists diagnose and treat conditions including stroke, epilepsy, Parkinson's disease, multiple sclerosis, sleep disorders, brain injury, migraine, brain tumors, and dementias.
wowFactor: Neurologists treat brain diseases without ever picking up a scalpel — that is the neurosurgeon's job, making them two completely different specialists for the same organ!

statement: The word 'neurology' comes from the Greek 'neûron,' originally meaning string or nerve, combined with '-logia,' meaning study of.
quizQuestion: What did the Greek root 'neûron' — source of neurology — originally mean?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, 'neurology' derives from the Greek 'neûron' (νεῦρον) meaning 'string, nerve' combined with the suffix '-logia' meaning 'study of.' The ancient Greeks originally used the word for string or sinew before applying it to nerve fibers.
wowFactor: The ancient Greeks called nerves strings — they literally saw the nervous system as invisible strings pulling your body like a puppet!

statement: Genetic factors may account for up to 70–90% of the risk of developing bipolar disorder, making it one of the most heritable psychiatric conditions.
quizQuestion: What is the upper estimated percentage of bipolar disorder risk from genetic factors?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, genetic factors may account for up to 70–90% of the risk of developing bipolar disorder, while environmental risks include a history of child abuse and long-term stress. This makes bipolar disorder one of the most heritable psychiatric conditions known.
wowFactor: Bipolar disorder is up to 90% genetic — more inherited than nearly any other psychiatric condition, making family history a massive risk signal.

statement: In bipolar disorder, 'mania' is severe elevated mood associated with psychosis, while 'hypomania' is milder and does not significantly affect daily functioning.
quizQuestion: When elevated mood in bipolar disorder includes psychosis, what is the episode called?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, if elevated mood in bipolar disorder is severe or associated with psychosis, it is called mania. If it does not significantly affect functioning, it is called hypomania. Bipolar I requires at least one manic episode; bipolar II requires at least one hypomanic episode and one major depressive episode.
wowFactor: Bipolar disorder has two flavors of high — hypomania can feel like a superpower, while mania crosses into psychosis and can upend a person's life within days.

statement: The central nervous system consists of the brain, spinal cord, and retina — the retina is technically CNS tissue, making your eyes an extension of your brain.
quizQuestion: Which surprising eye structure is officially part of the central nervous system?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the central nervous system (CNS) consists primarily of the brain, spinal cord, and retina. The retina — the light-sensitive tissue at the back of the eye — is technically CNS tissue, meaning your eyes are a literal anatomical extension of your brain.
wowFactor: Your eyes are literally brain tissue — the retina is officially part of the central nervous system, so you can see a tiny bit of your own brain by looking in a mirror!

statement: Only three animal groups have a true brain: arthropods, cephalopods, and vertebrates — all other animals have at most primitive brain precursors.
quizQuestion: Which invertebrate group shares a true brain with vertebrates?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, only arthropods, cephalopods, and vertebrates have a true brain, though precursor structures exist in onychophorans, gastropods, and lancelets. All other animal groups manage without a true centralized brain — some with no nervous system at all.
wowFactor: Octopuses are in an exclusive three-member club of animals with a real brain — most of Earth's animal diversity gets by without one!

statement: Alzheimer's disease accounts for 60–70% of all dementia cases worldwide, making it by far the most common form.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, Alzheimer's disease is the most common form of dementia, responsible for around 60–70% of cases. As of 2020, approximately 50 million people worldwide were living with the disease.
wowFactor: Nearly two out of every three dementia patients have Alzheimer's — it doesn't just lead the pack, it dwarfs all other causes of dementia combined.

statement: After an Alzheimer's diagnosis, average life expectancy ranges from 3 to 12 years — an unusually wide span for a medical prognosis.

wowFactor: An Alzheimer's diagnosis comes with a prognosis so uncertain — anywhere from 3 to 12 years — that doctors struggle to give patients any meaningful timeline.

statement: Alzheimer's disease affects women more often than men, despite affecting about 6% of all people over age 65.

wowFactor: Alzheimer's isn't an equal-opportunity disease — women are significantly more likely to develop it than men, a gap that researchers are still trying to fully explain.

statement: A single neuron's axon can travel up to 1 meter in length — running from the spinal cord all the way to a toe.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, a neuron's axon leaves the soma and travels for as far as 1 meter in humans — or even more in other species. This makes certain motor neurons among the longest single cells in the body.
wowFactor: A single nerve cell can be a full meter long — stretching from your spine to your toe, it is one of the longest individual cells in the entire human body.

statement: The ability to generate electric nerve signals first appeared in evolution 700–800 million years ago, during the Tonian period — before any complex animals existed.
quizQuestion: How long ago did the ability to generate electric nerve signals first evolve?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, molecular evidence suggests the ability to generate electric signals first appeared in evolution some 700 to 800 million years ago, during the Tonian period. Predecessors of neurons were peptidergic secretory cells that eventually gained new gene modules enabling fast electrical signaling.
wowFactor: Electrical nerve signals are older than complex animals — the circuitry in your brain traces back 700 million years, predating even the first worms.

statement: Some neurons lack dendrites entirely, and others have no axons — breaking the textbook rule that neurons always have both.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, neurons may lack dendrites or have no axons. The term 'neurite' is used to describe either a dendrite or an axon, particularly when the cell is undifferentiated. This structural flexibility allows for highly specialized signaling roles.
wowFactor: Some neurons have no dendrites — they're like a telephone that can only make calls but never receive them. Neural architecture is far stranger than textbooks suggest.

explanation: According to Wikidata, modifiable risk factors for dementia include smoking, obesity, sedentary lifestyle, social isolation, depression, arterial hypertension, alcohol abuse, diabetes, deafness, and air pollution. Targeting these through lifestyle changes can meaningfully reduce risk.

wowFactor: There's a psychiatric subspecialty called immuno-psychiatry — studying how your immune system can directly affect your mental health. Your brain and immune cells are in constant conversation.

statement: Alzheimer's disease accounts for 60–70% of all dementia cases, making it by far the most common cause.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, Alzheimer's is the most common form of dementia, responsible for 60–70% of all cases. It most often begins in people over 65, though up to 10% of cases are early-onset.
wowFactor: Nearly two-thirds of everyone with dementia has Alzheimer's — it is so dominant it is practically synonymous with the condition.

statement: Alzheimer's disease was named after Alois Alzheimer, the German psychiatrist who first described it in 1906.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, German psychiatrist and pathologist Alois Alzheimer first described the disease in 1906. The condition is now estimated to cost the global economy US$1 trillion annually.
wowFactor: The most feared brain disease of modern times was formally described over 120 years ago by a single German doctor — and we still have no cure.

statement: Alzheimer's disease is characterized by amyloid plaques and neurofibrillary tangles — misfolded protein deposits that destroy neurons.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, Alzheimer's progression is largely characterized by accumulation of amyloid plaques and neurofibrillary tangles in the cerebral cortex. These misfolded protein aggregates interfere with normal cell function and lead to irreversible neuron degeneration.
wowFactor: Alzheimer's is essentially a brain covered in sticky protein garbage — microscopic tangles and plaques that slowly strangle neurons over decades.

wowFactor: Alzheimer's costs the world a trillion dollars every single year — more than the entire GDP of most countries — and we still have no cure.

statement: When stressed, humans produce two major hormones: adrenaline (via the SAM axis) and cortisol (via the HPA axis).
quizQuestion: What are the two major hormones produced by humans during stressful situations?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, when stressed, humans rely on two major systems: the SAM axis releases adrenaline via the sympathetic nervous system (fight-or-flight), while the HPA axis regulates cortisol, which influences metabolic, psychological, and immunological functions.
wowFactor: Your body runs two completely separate stress systems simultaneously — one for instant fight-or-flight, another for longer-term hormonal adjustment.

statement: Chronic stress can alter memory functions, immune function, metabolism, and susceptibility to disease.
quizQuestion: Which body function can chronic stress impair alongside memory and metabolism?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, through the SAM and HPA axes, stress can alter memory functions, reward, immune function, metabolism, and susceptibility to diseases — making chronic stress a risk factor for many serious illnesses.
wowFactor: Chronic stress does not just make you feel bad — it literally rewires your memory, cripples your immune system, and changes how you metabolize food.

statement: The brain's stress response is regulated by five regions: the limbic system, prefrontal cortex, amygdala, hypothalamus, and stria terminalis.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, the SAM and HPA stress axes are regulated by several brain regions including the limbic system, prefrontal cortex, amygdala, hypothalamus, and stria terminalis. The amygdala is especially key to fear and emotional stress processing.
wowFactor: Your stress response is orchestrated by five different brain regions working simultaneously — it is a full-brain emergency committee firing at once.

statement: ADHD is primarily genetic, with a heritability rate of 70–80% according to meta-analyses.
quizQuestion: What is the heritability rate of ADHD according to meta-analyses?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, meta-analyses show ADHD is primarily genetic with a heritability rate of 70–80%, where risk factors are highly accumulative. Environmental risks exert their effects very early in life, in the prenatal or early postnatal period.
wowFactor: ADHD is more heritable than height — three-quarters of the risk is entirely in your genes, making it one of the most heritable psychiatric conditions known.

statement: ADHD can cause hyperfocus — an inability to stop an ongoing response even when the person intends to stop — the paradoxical opposite of inattention.
quizQuestion: What ADHD symptom causes a person to continue an action they intend to stop?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, inhibitory deficits in ADHD can cause difficulty interrupting an ongoing response, resulting in perseveration of actions despite intending to stop — known colloquially as hyperfocus. It is related to risks such as addiction.
wowFactor: ADHD's hyperfocus is the paradoxical opposite of distraction — people can become so locked into a task they literally cannot stop even when they want to.

statement: ADHD represents the extreme lower end of the bell curve of executive functioning — a continuous trait, not a discrete disorder.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, ADHD represents the extreme lower end of the continuous dimensional trait (bell curve) of executive functioning and self-regulation, supported by twin, brain imaging, and molecular genetic studies.
wowFactor: ADHD is not a separate brain type — it is just the far end of a single spectrum of executive function that includes everyone alive.

statement: The cerebellum's Latin name means 'little brain' — it fine-tunes motor coordination without initiating movement.
quizQuestion: What does the Latin word 'cerebellum' mean?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, 'cerebellum' is Latin for 'little brain'. It does not initiate movement but contributes to motor coordination, precision, and accurate timing — receiving sensory input from the spinal cord and other brain areas.
wowFactor: The cerebellum is literally called 'little brain' in Latin — and despite its modest size it is responsible for virtually all your movement precision.

wowFactor: There is a fish where the 'little brain' has grown so massive it actually outsizes the main brain — mormyrids are the outliers that break the universal rule.

statement: The cerebellar cortex is a thin accordion-folded layer containing Purkinje cells and granule cells arranged with remarkable regularity.

wowFactor: The cerebellum's surface is folded like an accordion — hidden inside are Purkinje cells so precisely arranged they look hand-drawn by an obsessive engineer.

statement: A coma involves the simultaneous loss of both wakefulness and awareness — both components required to maintain consciousness.
quizQuestion: What two components must be maintained for a person to remain conscious?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, for a patient to maintain consciousness, both wakefulness (a quantitative degree of consciousness) and awareness (a qualitative assessment of cognitive functions) must be maintained. Coma represents the failure of both.
wowFactor: Consciousness is not one thing — it is two: wakefulness and awareness. A coma kills both simultaneously, which is why a 'responsive-looking' coma patient may have zero inner experience.

statement: Comas can occur naturally or be medically induced — for example, during general anesthesia.
quizQuestion: What is one medical context in which a coma can be deliberately induced?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, comas can result from natural causes, or can be medically induced — for example, during general anesthesia. Coma patients often require extensive medical care to prevent complications such as pneumonia or blood clots.
wowFactor: Every time you go under general anesthesia, doctors deliberately put you into a controlled coma — then bring you back. It is the most routine medical miracle in modern surgery.

statement: The word 'coma' comes from Ancient Greek 'koma', meaning 'deep sleep'.
quizQuestion: What does the Ancient Greek word 'koma' — origin of 'coma' — mean?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, 'coma' derives from Ancient Greek κῶμα (koma), meaning 'deep sleep'. The medical state shares this metaphor: a patient cannot be awakened and fails to respond to stimuli.
wowFactor: The Greeks had a word for coma 2,500 years ago — 'koma' meaning deep sleep — because the unconscious resembles sleep so closely, yet the person is utterly unreachable.

statement: 'Neurosis' is no longer used in WHO or APA diagnostic manuals — it was officially retired from psychiatric diagnosis.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, 'neurosis' is no longer used in psychological disorder names by WHO's ICD or the APA's DSM. wowFactor: Neurosis — a word that defined a century of psychiatry — was quietly retired from official diagnosis. It still lives on as 'neuroticism,' a personality trait we all have to some degree.

statement: In 2015, encephalitis was estimated to have affected 4.3 million people and caused 150,000 deaths worldwide.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 2015, encephalitis was estimated to have affected 4.3 million people and resulted in 150,000 deaths worldwide. It is inflammation of the brain caused by viruses (including herpes simplex and rabies), bacteria, fungi, parasites, or autoimmune diseases.
wowFactor: Brain inflammation kills 150,000 people a year — more than many well-known diseases — yet most people do not even know what encephalitis is.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, causes of encephalitis include viruses (such as herpes simplex virus and rabies virus) as well as bacteria, fungi, or parasites. Other causes include autoimmune diseases and certain medications. In many cases the cause remains unknown.
wowFactor: Encephalitis can be triggered by anything from a common herpes virus to your own immune system accidentally inflaming your brain.

statement: Encephalitis symptoms include altered consciousness, aphasia, headache, fever, confusion, stiff neck, and vomiting — and may progress to seizures and hallucinations.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, encephalitis initial symptoms include headache, fever, confusion, and stiff neck. Complications can progress to seizures, hallucinations, trouble speaking, memory problems, and hearing difficulties. A weak immune system is a key risk factor.
wowFactor: Brain inflammation can escalate from a bad headache to full hallucinations and seizures — one of medicine's most dramatic escalating emergencies.

statement: Capsaicin, the compound that makes chili peppers hot, tricks the body's pain receptors into signalling burning heat — even when nothing is actually hot.
quizQuestion: What compound in chili peppers activates pain receptors to create a burning sensation?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, capsaicin and related capsaicinoids give chili peppers their pungency when ingested or applied topically. The compound binds to the TRPV1 receptor — the same receptor that detects actual heat — which is why eating a chili feels physically hot even at room temperature.
wowFactor: Eating a chili pepper feels exactly like touching a hot stove to your tongue — both trigger the identical pain receptor. Your brain is being tricked by a plant chemical!

variants.question: True or false: capsaicin activates the same pain receptors that detect actual heat.

statement: Before the late 16th century, chili peppers were unknown in Asia and Europe — European explorers brought them via the Columbian Exchange.
quizQuestion: How did chili peppers spread from the Americas to Asia and Europe?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, European explorers brought chili peppers to the Old World in the late 16th century as part of the Columbian Exchange — the massive transfer of plants, animals, and diseases between the Americas and the rest of the world after Columbus's voyages.
wowFactor: Thai food, Indian curry, Sichuan cuisine — all of them rely on chili peppers, yet none of those cultures had chili before the late 1500s. One ship changed global cuisine forever.

statement: Capsaicin from chili peppers is used in topical pain-relief creams — it depletes the nerve chemical that signals pain, numbing the area over time.
quizQuestion: How is capsaicin from chili peppers used in medicine?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, capsaicin and related capsaicinoids can be applied topically for traditional medicine and pain relief. Capsaicin creams work by initially stimulating and then depleting substance P — the chemical messenger that transmits pain signals — resulting in prolonged desensitisation.
wowFactor: The same chemical that makes chili peppers burn your mouth is deliberately smeared on skin to treat chronic pain — your body eventually runs out of the pain-signalling chemical!

variants.question: True or false: capsaicin can be applied to skin as a topical pain treatment.
Which plant compound is used in pain-relief creams applied to skin?
Why does repeated capsaicin application eventually reduce pain rather than increase it?

It triggers the release of endorphins

statement: A kangaroo joey spends about 7 months inside its mother's marsupium (pouch), completing development outside the womb.
quizQuestion: How long does a kangaroo joey spend developing inside the pouch?
explanation: According to Wikidata, kangaroo joeys spend approximately 7 months in the marsupium (pouch). Marsupials like kangaroos are born at a very early developmental stage — barely formed — and complete most of their development attached to a teat inside the pouch.
wowFactor: A newborn kangaroo is the size of a jellybean and has to crawl to the pouch unaided — it then spends 7 months in there finishing what other mammals do inside the womb.

variants.question: True or false: kangaroo joeys are born in a relatively undeveloped state and complete development in the pouch.

A large male kangaroo can be 2 metres tall and weigh up to ___ kg.

visualDescription: A cross-section diagram of a kangaroo pouch showing a tiny underdeveloped joey clinging to a teat inside, with a size comparison to a jellybean beside it.

statement: Kangaroos have teeth that move forward in their mouth as they wear down, eventually falling out and being replaced by new teeth growing from the back — like a conveyor belt.
quizQuestion: How do kangaroo molars replace themselves as they wear down?
correctAnswer: New teeth grow from the back
distractors: They grow back from the root
They are replaced all at once
They are shed and regrown like sharks
They dissolve and regrow from gum tissue
acceptableAnswers: move forward as new ones grow
explanation: According to Wikipedia, grass silica is highly abrasive, so kangaroo molars get ground down and actually move forward in the mouth before falling out, with new teeth growing in from the back. This process is called polyphyodonty and among mammals only occurs in kangaroos, elephants, and manatees.
wowFactor: Kangaroos have a built-in conveyor belt of teeth — worn-out molars shuffle forward and fall out while fresh ones queue up from the back. Only elephants and manatees share this trick!

variants.question: True or false: polyphyodonty — continuous tooth replacement from the back — also occurs in elephants and manatees.
Which animals share the kangaroo's unusual 'conveyor belt' tooth replacement system?
Why do kangaroos need such an advanced tooth replacement system?
variants.correctAnswer: Grass silica constantly grinds teeth down
distractors: Their teeth grow too fast otherwise
They chew bones as well as grass
They live far longer than other mammals

variants.question: The kangaroo tooth replacement process is called ___.

visualDescription: A cutaway X-ray of a kangaroo jaw shows a queue of replacement molars lined up from back to front, with worn teeth at the very front about to fall out.

statement: Woolly mammoths survived on Russia's Wrangel Island until about 4,000 years ago — the same era as ancient Egypt and Mesopotamia's earliest civilisations.
quizQuestion: How recently did the last woolly mammoths survive on Wrangel Island?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, woolly mammoths survived on Russia's Wrangel Island in the Arctic Ocean until as recently as 4,000 years ago — a time when ancient Egypt and Mesopotamia already had advanced civilisations, pyramids, and writing.
wowFactor: While Egyptians were building pyramids and writing hieroglyphs, mammoths were still alive on a Russian island. They're not nearly as ancient as you think!

visualDescription: A split scene: Egyptian pharaohs building a pyramid on one side, a shaggy woolly mammoth grazing a frozen Arctic island on the other — both in the same historical moment.

statement: The woolly mammoth evolved about 400,000–700,000 years ago in Siberia, developing thick fur and other cold-weather adaptations.
quizQuestion: Where did woolly mammoths first evolve?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the woolly mammoth evolved about 700,000–400,000 years ago in Siberia. The species developed numerous adaptations to cold environments, including a thick layer of fur and spirally twisted tusks, distinguishing them from living elephants.
wowFactor: The woolly mammoth didn't evolve in a freezing wilderness — it evolved and then adapted to the cold, developing its famous shaggy coat over hundreds of thousands of years in Siberia.

variants.question: True or false: mammoths are more closely related to Asian elephants than to African elephants.

statement: The cerebrum controls all voluntary actions in the human body — with the cerebellum's assistance — making it the command center for every deliberate movement you make.
quizQuestion: Which part of the brain, with the cerebellum's help, controls all voluntary actions in the body?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, with the assistance of the cerebellum, the cerebrum controls all voluntary actions in the human body. The cerebrum is the uppermost region of the central nervous system and develops prenatally from the forebrain.
wowFactor: Every deliberate movement you've ever made — kicking a ball, writing your name, tying your shoes — was commanded by your cerebrum, the brain's oversized control tower!

variants.question: True or false: the cerebrum controls all voluntary actions in the human body.
Which largest brain region controls all voluntary actions with cerebellar assistance?
The cerebrum develops prenatally from which embryonic brain region?
The cerebrum is divided into approximately symmetric left and right cerebral ___.

statement: The human brain contains more than 86 billion neurons — roughly equal to the number of non-neuronal support cells — all packed into an organ weighing about 1.2 kg.
quizQuestion: Approximately how many neurons are in the human brain?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, there are more than 86 billion neurons in the human brain, and a more or less equal number of other cells. The brain weighs approximately 1.2 kg according to Wikidata — making it one of the densest and most complex information-processing structures known.
wowFactor: Your brain has more neurons than the Milky Way has stars visible to the naked eye — 86 billion tiny cells, each connecting to thousands of others, all packed into 1.2 kilograms!

variants.question: True or false: the number of neurons in the human brain roughly equals the number of non-neuronal cells.
Approximately how much does the human brain weigh?
The human brain consists of the cerebrum, the cerebellum, and what third major structure?
Brain activity is made possible by neuron interconnections and their release of ___.

visualDescription: A human brain floats on a dark background, each neuron lighting up as a tiny star — 86 billion points of light forming constellations of thought inside 1.2 kilograms of tissue.

statement: The human brain is isolated from the bloodstream by the blood-brain barrier — a selective molecular wall that protects it from toxins and pathogens while allowing essential nutrients through.
quizQuestion: What specialized barrier isolates the human brain from the bloodstream?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the brain is protected by the skull, suspended in cerebrospinal fluid, and isolated from the bloodstream by the blood-brain barrier. This barrier is what makes many drugs unable to treat brain conditions — they cannot cross into the brain.
wowFactor: Your brain has its own exclusive security system — the blood-brain barrier stops almost everything in your bloodstream from entering, which is why most drugs can't treat brain diseases directly!

variants.question: True or false: the brain is suspended in cerebrospinal fluid inside the skull.
What makes many drugs ineffective against brain diseases even when they work elsewhere in the body?
The brain's main arterial blood supply includes the internal carotid artery and which circular structure?
The brain's venous blood drains primarily through the ___ jugular vein.

visualDescription: A cross-section of a brain capillary: red blood cells flowing through a vessel while a molecular barrier glows yellow, blocking toxins on one side while tiny nutrient molecules slip through selective gates.

statement: The brainstem makes up only about 2.6% of the brain's total weight yet controls heart rate, breathing rate, and the sleep cycle — life depends on this tiny stalk.
quizQuestion: What percentage of total brain weight does the brainstem make up?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the brainstem is very small, making up around only 2.6 percent of the brain's total weight. Despite this, it has the critical roles of regulating heart rate and breathing rate, providing cranial nerve supply to the face and neck, and regulating the sleep cycle.
wowFactor: The brainstem is only 2.6% of your brain by weight — yet damage to this tiny stalk stops your heart, your breathing, and your sleep all at once. The smallest part has the biggest job!

variants.question: True or false: the brainstem regulates both heart rate and breathing rate.
Which brain structure controls heart rate, breathing, and the sleep cycle despite being only 2.6% of brain weight?
How many pairs of cranial nerves originate from the brainstem?
The human brainstem is composed of the midbrain, the pons, and the medulla ___.

visualDescription: A brain diagram with the large cerebrum grey and the tiny brainstem glowing red at the base — labeled '2.6%' — with arrows pointing to a beating heart, breathing lungs, and a sleeping figure.

statement: Ten of the twelve pairs of cranial nerves originate directly from the brainstem, providing all sensory and motor nerve supply to the face and neck.
quizQuestion: How many pairs of cranial nerves originate from the brainstem?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, ten pairs of cranial nerves come from the brainstem. The brainstem provides the main motor and sensory nerve supply to the face and neck via these cranial nerves — a critical communication hub between the brain and the head.
wowFactor: Ten of your twelve cranial nerve pairs stream directly out of the brainstem — that tiny 2.6% stalk is running most of the wiring in your face, neck, tongue, and throat!

variants.question: True or false: ten of the twelve cranial nerve pairs originate from the brainstem.
Which brain structure is the origin of ten of the twelve cranial nerve pairs?

The brainstem includes the midbrain, the ___, and the medulla oblongata.

statement: The six-layered neocortex makes up approximately 90% of the cerebral cortex and is the most recently evolved part of the brain, responsible for higher thought and language.
quizQuestion: What percentage of the cerebral cortex does the six-layered neocortex comprise?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the six-layered neocortex makes up approximately 90% of the cortex, with the allocortex making up the remainder. The cerebral cortex plays a key role in attention, perception, awareness, thought, memory, language, and consciousness.
wowFactor: The part of your brain that lets you read this sentence, feel empathy, and plan the future — the neocortex — makes up 90% of your cortex and is what sets human brains apart from most animals!

variants.question: True or false: the neocortex makes up approximately 90% of the human cerebral cortex.
Which brain layer, comprising 90% of the cortex, is responsible for consciousness and language?

statement: The human cerebral cortex is so heavily folded that the majority of its surface is hidden inside the sulci (grooves) — folding allows a huge surface area to fit inside the skull.
quizQuestion: Why is the human cerebral cortex folded rather than smooth?
distractors: To separate left and right hemispheres
To cool the brain more efficiently
To create spaces for cerebrospinal fluid
acceptableAnswers: pack more area in limited space
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in most mammals, apart from small mammals with small brains, the cerebral cortex is folded, providing a greater surface area in the confined volume of the cranium. In the human brain, the majority of the cerebral cortex is not visible from the outside but buried in the sulci.
wowFactor: If you unfolded a human cerebral cortex and laid it flat, it would be roughly the size of a pillowcase — all of that crammed into a skull through billions of years of folding evolution!

variants.question: True or false: in the human brain, most of the cerebral cortex is hidden inside the sulci rather than visible on the surface.

statement: Secondary (metastatic) brain tumors are about four times as common as primary brain tumors — meaning most brain cancers actually started somewhere else in the body.
quizQuestion: How much more common are secondary (metastatic) brain tumors than primary brain tumors?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, secondary or metastatic brain tumors are about four times as common as primary brain tumors, with about half of metastases coming from lung cancer. Primary brain tumors occur in around 250,000 people a year globally.
wowFactor: Most brain cancer didn't start in the brain — secondary tumors spreading from elsewhere are four times more common, and half of those come from lung cancer!

statement: In children younger than 15, brain tumors are the second most common form of cancer — second only to acute lymphoblastic leukemia.
quizQuestion: In children under 15, what is brain cancer's rank among the most common cancers?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in children younger than 15, brain tumors are second only to acute lymphoblastic leukemia as the most common form of cancer. Primary brain tumors make up less than 2% of all cancers overall.
wowFactor: Brain tumors are the second most common childhood cancer — and among all cancers in any age group, brain cancer has one of the highest economic costs per case due to treatment complexity.

statement: Unlike the brain and spinal cord, the peripheral nervous system has no protection from bone or blood-brain barrier — leaving it exposed to toxins and physical injury.
quizQuestion: What makes the peripheral nervous system more vulnerable to toxins than the central nervous system?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, unlike the central nervous system, the peripheral nervous system is not protected by the vertebral column and skull, or by the blood-brain barrier, which leaves it exposed to toxins. This is why certain poisons and diseases attack the peripheral nervous system more readily.
wowFactor: Your brain and spinal cord sit in armored bone with a molecular forcefield — but the rest of your nervous system is exposed to everything in your body, including poisons and pathogens!

statement: Intellectual disability affects about 2-3% of the general population, with 75-90% of those affected having the mild form.
quizQuestion: What percentage of people with intellectual disability have the mild form?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, intellectual disability affects about 2-3% of the general population. Seventy-five to ninety percent of the affected people have mild intellectual disability. About a quarter of cases are caused by a genetic disorder.
wowFactor: Intellectual disability is far more common than most people realize — and 75-90% of those affected have the mild form, meaning many live independently and are barely distinguishable from the general population.

statement: Every neuron in the nervous system has an axon — the long stem-like part that fires action potentials to transmit signals from one cell to the next.
quizQuestion: What is the long stem-like part of a neuron that sends electrical signals to the next cell?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, nervous tissue is made up of different types of neurons, all of which have an axon — the long stem-like part of the cell that sends action potentials to the next cell. Bundles of axons make up the nerves in the PNS and the tracts in the CNS.
wowFactor: Every single one of your 86 billion neurons has an axon — a biological wire that fires electrical pulses called action potentials. Bundle enough of them and you get a nerve!

variants.question: True or false: bundles of axons make up the nerves in the peripheral nervous system.

statement: The cerebrum is the largest part of the human brain and controls all voluntary actions in the body.
quizQuestion: Which part of the brain is the largest and controls all voluntary actions?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the cerebrum (also called telencephalon or endbrain) is the largest part of the brain, containing the cerebral cortex. With the assistance of the cerebellum, it controls all voluntary actions in the human body.
wowFactor: The cerebrum is the massive wrinkled dome of your brain — and it's running every deliberate action you take, from typing to dancing to reading this card!

statement: The cerebrum is divided into approximately symmetric left and right cerebral hemispheres, each with distinct functions.
quizQuestion: Into how many approximate mirror-image halves is the cerebrum divided?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the cerebrum is divided into approximately symmetric left and right cerebral hemispheres. It contains the cerebral cortex, as well as several subcortical structures including the hippocampus, basal ganglia, and olfactory bulb.
wowFactor: Your brain has two nearly mirror-image halves — but they're far from identical in function, with language, logic, and creativity divided unevenly between them!

statement: Unlike the brain and spinal cord, the peripheral nervous system is not protected by bone or the blood-brain barrier — leaving it exposed to toxins.
quizQuestion: Which nervous system division lacks the protection of bone and the blood-brain barrier?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, unlike the central nervous system, the peripheral nervous system (PNS) is not protected by the vertebral column, skull, or blood-brain barrier, leaving it exposed to toxins and physical damage.
wowFactor: Your brain sits inside a fortified castle of bone and a chemical barrier — but the nerves in your limbs have zero such protection and are directly exposed to toxins!

statement: The autonomic division of the peripheral nervous system controls smooth muscle and glands involuntarily — without conscious thought.
quizQuestion: Which nervous system division controls your heartbeat and glands without conscious input?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the autonomic nervous system — part of the peripheral nervous system — exerts involuntary control over smooth muscle and glands. It operates without conscious thought, regulating heartbeat, digestion, and other automatic body processes.
wowFactor: Your autonomic nervous system keeps you alive 24/7 without you ever thinking about it — managing your heartbeat, breathing, and digestion completely on autopilot!

statement: The somatosensory cortex contains a map of the entire body — neighbouring neurons represent neighbouring skin locations.
quizQuestion: What is the name for the brain's internal body map in the somatosensory cortex?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, neighbouring neurons in the somatosensory cortex represent nearby locations on the skin or in the body, creating a map called the sensory homunculus. This preserves information about where a physical stimulus occurred.
wowFactor: Your brain has a distorted cartoon map of your entire body — areas with more touch sensitivity (like lips and hands) take up far more brain space than your back!

statement: Touch is classified as a 'general somatic sense' — distinct from the five special senses of sight, smell, taste, hearing, and balance.
quizQuestion: Which of the following is classified as a 'special sense' rather than a general somatic sense?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the five special senses are sight, smell, taste, hearing, and balance. Touch — including light touch, vibration, pressure, and tension — is classified as a general somatic sense, not a special sense.
wowFactor: Most people say we have five senses, but touch is actually categorised separately — it's a 'general somatic sense' while sight, smell, taste, hearing, and balance are the true 'special senses'!

variants.question: True or false: balance is classified as one of the five special senses.

statement: Fewer than 10% of sports-related concussions in children involve loss of consciousness — most concussions happen without blacking out.
quizQuestion: What percentage of children's sports concussions involve loss of consciousness?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, fewer than 10% of sports-related concussions among children are associated with loss of consciousness. Symptoms typically appear within minutes to hours and may last 2 weeks in adults and 4 weeks in children.
wowFactor: Most concussions never knock anyone out — fewer than 1 in 10 sports head injuries involve loss of consciousness, yet they still cause weeks of symptoms!

statement: Current concussion treatment limits complete rest to just 1-2 days — prolonged rest actually slows recovery and worsens depression.
quizQuestion: How many days of complete rest are now recommended after a concussion?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, treatment of concussion includes relative rest for no more than 1-2 days, followed by aerobic exercise to increase heart rate. Prolonged periods of rest may slow recovery and result in greater depression and anxiety.
wowFactor: Old advice said rest completely after a concussion — but science now shows that after just 1-2 days, getting moving actually speeds up recovery!

statement: Stimulants have been used since at least 8000 BCE — archaeological evidence from Peru shows cocaine use dating back that far.
quizQuestion: Archaeological evidence from Peru shows cocaine use dating back to around what year BCE?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, archaeological evidence from Peru shows that cocaine use dates back as far as 8000 BCE, making stimulant use one of the oldest documented human behaviours. Stimulants increase activity in the sympathetic nervous system.
wowFactor: Humans have been using stimulants for at least 10,000 years — Peruvian coca leaves are among the oldest documented drugs in human history!

variants.question: True or false: stimulants have been in documented use for at least 8,000 years.

statement: Caffeine works not by directly stimulating the brain, but by blocking adenosine — the chemical that promotes sleep.
quizQuestion: How does caffeine produce its stimulating effect on the brain?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, caffeine works by blocking the activity of endogenous agents that promote sleep — specifically adenosine. Other stimulants like methylphenidate work differently, by increasing synaptic concentrations of norepinephrine and dopamine.
wowFactor: Caffeine doesn't make you more awake — it just stops you from feeling tired by blocking the 'sleepiness molecule' adenosine. Remove the block and the tiredness floods back!

variants.question: True or false: caffeine works by blocking adenosine, the chemical that promotes sleep.

statement: Because of neuroplasticity, the brain can partially recover from damage by forming new neural connections to compensate for injured areas.
quizQuestion: What property allows the brain to form new connections to compensate for damaged areas?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, because of neuroplasticity, the brain can partially recover function by forming new neural connections to compensate for damaged areas. Patients may regain adaptive skills such as movement and speech, especially with therapy and practice.
wowFactor: Your brain can rewire itself after injury — neuroplasticity lets healthy neurons pick up the work of damaged ones, giving stroke and TBI survivors a real chance at recovery!

variants.question: True or false: the brain can form new neural connections to compensate for damage.

statement: A patient in a vegetative state for four or more weeks is classified as being in a persistent vegetative state (PVS).
quizQuestion: After how many weeks in a vegetative state is a patient classified as having a persistent vegetative state?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, after four weeks in a vegetative state, the patient is classified as being in a persistent vegetative state (PVS). It is further classified as permanent after three months (non-traumatic) or one year (traumatic injury) in the US.
wowFactor: The line between 'vegetative state' and 'persistent vegetative state' is simply four weeks — a clinical threshold with profound implications for treatment decisions!

variants.question: True or false: a patient must be in a vegetative state for at least 4 weeks to be classified as persistent.

statement: Medicine has been practiced since prehistoric times — for most of human history it was an art combining herbs, prayers, and philosophical theories like the 'four humors.'
quizQuestion: What ancient medical theory divided health into four bodily fluids, guiding treatment for centuries?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, medicine has been practiced since prehistoric times, often connected to religious and philosophical beliefs. Ancient philosopher-physicians applied bloodletting according to 'humorism' — the theory of the four humors — long before modern science emerged.
wowFactor: For thousands of years, doctors drained blood from sick patients based on the 'four humors' theory — an approach that probably killed more people than it cured!

statement: In 2024, tuberculosis caused an estimated 1.23 million deaths worldwide, making it the leading cause of death from a single infectious disease — surpassing HIV/AIDS.
quizQuestion: Which infectious disease caused the most deaths worldwide in 2024?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 2024 tuberculosis is estimated to have newly infected 10.7 million people and caused 1.23 million deaths, making it the leading cause of death from an infectious disease. It has been called the white death and historically known as consumption.
wowFactor: Tuberculosis — an ancient disease called consumption — still kills 1.23 million people a year, making it the world's deadliest infectious disease, ahead of HIV/AIDS.

statement: In the 1800s, tuberculosis — then called consumption — was responsible for an estimated one quarter of all deaths in Europe.

wowFactor: In 19th century Europe, one in four people who died was killed by tuberculosis. It was so common it shaped literature, art, and the romantic ideal of the pale, thin intellectual.

statement: Malaria is caused not by a virus or bacterium but by single-celled parasites called Plasmodium, transmitted through the bites of infected female Anopheles mosquitoes.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, malaria is caused by single-celled parasites of the genus Plasmodium, spread through bites of infected female Anopheles mosquitoes. P. falciparum is responsible for the vast majority of malaria deaths.
wowFactor: Malaria is not caused by a virus or bacterium — it is caused by a tiny parasite that hijacks both a mosquito and your red blood cells in two stages of its life cycle.

statement: Around 95% of all malaria cases and deaths occur in sub-Saharan Africa, with 263 million cases and 597,000 deaths recorded worldwide in 2023.
