statement: Once rabies symptoms appear, the disease is virtually always fatal — yet a vaccine given before symptoms develop is almost entirely effective at preventing death.
quizQuestion: What is the survival rate once rabies symptoms have appeared in an untreated patient?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, once symptoms appear the result is virtually always death. As of 2016, only fourteen people worldwide had been documented to survive rabies after showing symptoms. However, post-exposure prophylaxis given before symptom onset is highly effective.
wowFactor: Rabies is one of the few diseases that is virtually 100% fatal once symptoms appear — yet the vaccine works perfectly if given in time. As of 2016, only 14 people had ever survived it with symptoms.

wowFactor: Dogs are responsible for over 99% of human rabies deaths in countries where the disease circulates in dog populations. Eliminating dog rabies would effectively end human rabies worldwide.

statement: COVID-19 was first identified in Wuhan, China in December 2019. The WHO declared it a global health emergency in March 2020 and declared the end of the emergency in May 2023.

explanation: According to Wikipedia and Wikidata, the first known COVID-19 case was identified in Wuhan, China in December 2019. The disease spread worldwide starting January 2020, and the WHO declared a global health emergency in March 2020, ending it in May 2023.
wowFactor: COVID-19 went from a single cluster in one city in December 2019 to a global pandemic affecting nearly every country within three months. The fastest spread of any pandemic in recorded history.

statement: A person with HIV on effective antiretroviral treatment can have an undetectable viral load, meaning they have effectively no risk of transmitting HIV sexually.

statement: Cholera kills through extreme dehydration, but its death rate drops from up to 50% to below 5% with the simple treatment of oral rehydration salts — sugar, salt, and water.
quizQuestion: What simple treatment dramatically reduces cholera death rates from up to 50% to below 5%?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the primary treatment for cholera is oral rehydration salts (ORS) — replacement of fluids and electrolytes using slightly sweet and salty solutions. The risk of death is usually below 5% with improved treatment, but may reach 50% without access.
wowFactor: Cholera kills by dehydration. The treatment is essentially sugar water with a pinch of salt. Yet it drops the death rate from 50% to below 5% — one of medicine's greatest wins.

statement: Since the late 1800s, pandemic outbreaks of novel influenza strains have occurred every 10 to 50 years, with five flu pandemics occurring since 1900.

Five flu pandemics have occurred since 1900: the 1918 Spanish flu (most severe), the 1957 Asian flu, the 1968 Hong Kong flu, the 1977 Russian flu, and the 2009 H1N1 pandemic.
wowFactor: The flu is not just an annual nuisance — major pandemic strains hit every few decades. We have had five since 1900, and the 1918 Spanish flu killed more people than World War I.

statement: The word cancer comes from the Latin and Greek word for crab — ancient physicians named it after a crab because tumors, with their hard center and radiating vessels, resembled a crab's body.
quizQuestion: Which creature gave cancer its name in ancient Greek and Latin medicine?
explanation: According to Wikidata, cancer is named after the crab (Crustacea). Ancient physicians — including Hippocrates — named tumors after the crab because the hard center of a tumor with radiating blood vessels resembled a crab's body and legs.
wowFactor: Cancer gets its name from the Latin word for crab. When ancient Greek doctors saw a tumor with blood vessels spreading out from its center, it looked just like a crab — and the name stuck for 2,000 years.

statement: Vaccines work by training the immune system to recognize and destroy a specific pathogen without causing the actual disease.
quizQuestion: How does a vaccine protect against disease without causing the illness itself?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, a vaccine typically contains an agent resembling a disease-causing microorganism and stimulates the immune system to recognize the agent as a threat, destroy it, and remember it for future encounters — providing protection without causing the disease.
wowFactor: A vaccine is essentially a wanted poster for your immune system — it shows your body what the enemy looks like so it is ready to fight the real thing instantly.

wowFactor: Smallpox killed around 300 million people in the 20th century alone — then vaccination wiped it off the face of the Earth entirely. It no longer exists in the wild.

statement: The word "vaccine" comes from the Latin word for cow, because the first vaccine used cowpox to protect against smallpox.

wowFactor: Every vaccine ever made carries a cow's name — Edward Jenner named vaccination after the Latin "vacca" (cow) because he used cowpox scabs to defeat smallpox in 1798.

statement: The primary treatment for cholera is oral rehydration therapy — a simple slightly sweet and salty solution that has saved millions of lives.

statement: Antibiotics are effective only against bacteria — they do not work against viral infections such as the common cold or influenza.

statement: The word "antibiotic" literally means "opposing life" — from the Greek roots anti (against) and bios (life).

statement: The adaptive immune system learns to recognise specific pathogens it has encountered before, creating immunological memory that makes vaccines possible.
quizQuestion: What immune system property makes vaccines work by training the body to fight future infections?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the adaptive immune system provides a tailored response to each pathogen by learning to recognise molecules it has previously encountered. This immunological memory is the fundamental basis of vaccination.
wowFactor: Your immune system is a living library — every pathogen it defeats gets filed away, so if it ever attacks again, your body destroys it before you even feel sick!

statement: According to the WHO, vaccination prevents 3.5 to 5 million deaths per year worldwide, and over a 50-year period starting in 1974, vaccines prevented 154 million deaths.
quizQuestion: How many deaths does the WHO estimate vaccination prevents each year worldwide?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the World Health Organization reports that vaccination prevents 3.5-5 million deaths per year. A Lancet study cited by Wikipedia estimates that over the 50-year period starting in 1974, vaccination prevented 154 million deaths, including 146 million among children under age 5.
wowFactor: Vaccines have saved 154 million lives in just 50 years — that is more than the entire population of Russia, rescued from preventable diseases!

statement: Smallpox is the only human infectious disease ever fully eradicated — it was declared eliminated in 1979 after killing an estimated 300-500 million people in the 20th century alone.
quizQuestion: Smallpox is the only infectious disease to be fully eradicated. When was it declared eliminated?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, smallpox was finally eradicated in 1979 through a global vaccination campaign. It had already killed an estimated 300-500 million people in the 20th century alone, and caused deaths in 20-60% of infected adults and over 80% of infected children.
wowFactor: Smallpox killed half a billion people in the 20th century alone — then humanity eradicated it entirely with vaccines, making it the greatest medical achievement in history!

statement: The common cold can be caused by over 200 different virus strains, which is why no vaccine has ever been developed for it.
quizQuestion: Why has no vaccine ever been developed for the common cold?
distractors: The virus mutates too fast to study
The body cures itself too fast
acceptableAnswers: too many different viruses cause it
explanation: According to Wikipedia, well over 200 virus strains are implicated in causing the common cold, with rhinoviruses, coronaviruses, and adenoviruses being the most common. The rapid mutation and wide variation of causative viruses makes a universal vaccine unfeasible.
wowFactor: There is no cold vaccine because "the cold" is actually over 200 different viruses — vaccinating against all of them would be like trying to hit 200 moving targets at once!

statement: Ebola virus disease has a mortality rate of 25-90%, averaging around 50%, and was first reported in 1976 near the Ebola River in the Democratic Republic of Congo.
quizQuestion: What is the average mortality rate of Ebola virus disease?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Ebola causes a mortality rate of anywhere between 25 and 90%, averaging approximately 50%. The disease was first reported in 1976 and is named after the Ebola River in the Democratic Republic of the Congo where it was discovered.
wowFactor: Ebola kills roughly half of everyone it infects — yet fruit bats carry and spread it without being harmed at all, acting as the natural reservoir for one of the deadliest viruses on Earth!

statement: Leprosy is not highly contagious — 95% of people who are exposed to the bacteria Mycobacterium leprae never develop the disease.
quizQuestion: What percentage of people exposed to the leprosy bacterium never develop the disease?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, leprosy has a low pathogenicity, and 95% of people who are exposed to Mycobacterium leprae do not develop the disease. Despite this, historical stigma led to forced separation in leper colonies, a practice still occurring in some areas despite lacking scientific support.
wowFactor: Leprosy — history's most feared disease — is so weakly contagious that 95% of people cannot even catch it, yet millions were locked in colonies for centuries because of this myth!

statement: Aspirin's active compound derives from salicin found in willow bark, which has been used medicinally for at least 2,400 years, long before aspirin itself was synthesised in 1897.
quizQuestion: Aspirin's predecessor compound, salicin, was found in the bark of which tree?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, a precursor to aspirin found in the bark of the willow tree (genus Salix), salicin, is metabolised into the medicinally active compound salicylic acid and has been used for its health effects for at least 2,400 years. Felix Hoffmann of Bayer produced aspirin in a pure, stable form in 1897.
wowFactor: The painkiller in every medicine cabinet is just a refined version of willow bark — a remedy that ancient Greeks used 2,400 years ago and that is still the world's most consumed drug!

statement: An allergy is an exaggerated immune response where the body mistakenly identifies a harmless allergen as a threat, triggering histamine release.
quizQuestion: What chemical does the immune system release during an allergic reaction, causing sneezing, itching, and swelling?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, allergic reactions involve immunoglobulin E antibodies (IgE) binding to an allergen and then to a receptor on mast cells or basophils, where they trigger the release of inflammatory chemicals such as histamine. This causes the familiar symptoms of hay fever, rashes, and anaphylaxis.
wowFactor: Allergy symptoms — sneezing, itching, swelling — are not caused by the pollen or peanut itself, but by your own immune system over-reacting to something completely harmless!

statement: Dengue virus has four distinct serotypes, and catching one type gives lifelong immunity to that type — but a second infection with a different type increases the risk of life-threatening complications.
quizQuestion: How many distinct serotypes of dengue virus exist, each requiring separate immunity?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the dengue virus has four confirmed serotypes. Infection with one type usually gives lifelong immunity to that type but only short-term immunity to the others. Subsequent infection with a different serotype increases the risk of severe complications through antibody-dependent enhancement.
wowFactor: Surviving dengue once actually makes the next infection MORE dangerous — antibodies from the first type accidentally help the second type infect you faster!

statement: Without treatment, average survival after HIV infection is 9-11 years; with modern antiretroviral therapy, a person can live a nearly normal lifespan.
quizQuestion: With modern antiretroviral treatment, someone living with HIV can expect to live how long?
acceptableAnswers: nearly the same as uninfected people
explanation: According to Wikipedia, without treatment the average survival time after HIV infection is estimated to be 9-11 years. However, an HIV-positive person on antiretroviral treatment can expect to live a nearly standard life expectancy — dying with the virus, not of it.
wowFactor: HIV went from a death sentence in the 1980s to a manageable chronic condition today — modern treatment means someone diagnosed now can expect to live a normal lifespan!

statement: Chickenpox and shingles are caused by the same virus — varicella zoster virus (VZV) — which lies dormant after the initial infection and can reactivate decades later as shingles.
quizQuestion: Chickenpox and shingles are both caused by the same virus. Which virus is it?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, chickenpox (varicella) is caused by varicella zoster virus (VZV), a member of the herpesvirus family. The connection between chickenpox and shingles was determined in 1888: the same virus lies dormant in nerve tissue after chickenpox and can reactivate as shingles later in life.
wowFactor: The itchy childhood rash of chickenpox never fully leaves — the virus hides in your nerve cells for decades and can come back as the agonising shingles rash when you're older!

statement: Following vaccines in the 1950s, wild polio cases fell from an estimated 350,000 in 1988 to just 30 confirmed cases in 2022.

wowFactor: Vaccines reduced a disease that paralysed 350,000 children a year down to just 30 cases — one of medicine's most extraordinary achievements!

wowFactor: Most people know about A, B, AB, and O blood types — but doctors actually track 48 different blood group systems in total!

statement: Lung cancer is the most diagnosed and deadliest cancer worldwide, with 2.2 million cases and 1.8 million deaths in 2020.

statement: The human immune system has two major subsystems: the innate immune system (fast, generic) and the adaptive immune system (slow, tailored, and memory-forming) — the latter being the basis of all vaccines.
quizQuestion: Which part of the immune system forms memory of past pathogens and is the basis for vaccines?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the immune system has two major subsystems: the innate system provides a preconfigured response to broad threats, while the adaptive system tailors its response by learning to recognize specific pathogens encountered previously. This adaptive immunological memory is the fundamental basis of how vaccines work.
wowFactor: Vaccines work by tricking your adaptive immune system into 'remembering' a pathogen it has never actually faced — giving you immunity without the disease.

statement: At any given time, nearly 70% of the total volume of blood in the human body is held inside the veins — earning them the name 'capacitance vessels'.
quizQuestion: What percentage of total blood volume is held in the veins at any given time?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, veins have wider lumens and thinner walls than arteries, giving them a greater capacity to expand and hold blood. At any time, nearly 70% of total blood volume is in the veins, which is why they are called 'capacitance vessels.' Venous valves prevent backflow, especially in the lower limbs.
wowFactor: Almost three-quarters of all your blood is sitting in your veins right now — arteries are the highways, but veins are the parking lots of the circulatory system!

statement: Multiple sclerosis (MS) is an autoimmune disease where the immune system attacks myelin — the insulating cover of nerve cells — disrupting signals throughout the central nervous system.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, multiple sclerosis is an autoimmune disease where the immune system attacks myelin, the insulating cover of nerve cells. wowFactor: In MS, your own immune system attacks the insulation around your nerve wires — like a fire that eats through electrical cables, causing short-circuits throughout the brain and spine!

statement: The spleen acts primarily as a blood filter — it removes old red blood cells, holds a reserve of blood, recycles iron, and acts as a key site of immune activity.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, the spleen acts primarily as a blood filter — it removes old red blood cells, holds a reserve of blood useful in hemorrhagic shock, and recycles iron. As part of the mononuclear phagocyte system, it also plays a key role in immune function and stores monocytes that respond to tissue injury.
wowFactor: The spleen is your body's blood recycling plant — it dismantles old red blood cells, salvages the iron, and keeps a blood reserve on standby for emergencies!

statement: Eyes with resolving power have evolved in ten fundamentally different forms across the animal kingdom.

wowFactor: Eyes evolved independently at least 10 different times — yet most end up solving the same light-sensing problem in completely different ways!

statement: The eye performs photo-response functions independent of vision, including regulating the body's circadian rhythm via the retinohypothalamic tract.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, retinal photosensitive ganglion cells send signals along the retinohypothalamic tract to the suprachiasmatic nuclei to effect circadian adjustment — completely separate from the visual pathway used for sight. This is why exposure to bright light at night disrupts sleep.
wowFactor: Your eyes do a second hidden job: syncing your body clock. Special retinal cells bypass your visual cortex entirely and talk directly to your brain's day-night timer.

wowFactor: Your pupil shrinks in bright light automatically via a brain region completely separate from where you actually see — the reflex bypasses your consciousness entirely.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, a neuron's axon leaves the soma at the axon hillock and can travel up to 1 meter in humans — and even longer in other species. This allows a single nerve cell to run from the spinal cord all the way down to the foot.
wowFactor: A single nerve cell in your body can be a meter long — stretching from your spine all the way to your toe!

statement: The ability to generate electrical signals in nerve cells first appeared in evolution approximately 700 to 800 million years ago during the Tonian period.
quizQuestion: How many million years ago did electrical signaling in nerve cells first evolve?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, molecular evidence suggests the ability to generate electrical signals first appeared around 700 to 800 million years ago during the Tonian period. The predecessors of neurons were peptidergic secretory cells that eventually gained new gene modules enabling fast electrical signals.
wowFactor: Your ability to think traces back 700 million years — to single cells that learned to zap each other with electricity before complex animals even existed!

statement: A key warning sign of meningitis is a non-blanching rash — one that does not fade when a glass is rolled over it.
quizQuestion: What household item is used to test for a dangerous meningitis rash?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, one warning sign of meningitis is a non-blanching rash — a rash that does NOT fade when a glass is rolled over it. This simple test can be lifesaving, as meningitis is classified as a medical emergency. Rashes that do fade under pressure are usually less dangerous.
wowFactor: Rolling a drinking glass over a rash can tell you if someone has a life-threatening meningitis infection — a simple trick that has saved thousands of lives!

statement: Outbreaks of bacterial meningitis occur each year between December and June in a region of sub-Saharan Africa known as the meningitis belt.
quizQuestion: What is the African region with annual bacterial meningitis outbreaks called?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, bacterial meningitis outbreaks occur every year between December and June in a swath of sub-Saharan Africa known as the meningitis belt. In 2019, meningitis was diagnosed in about 7.7 million people worldwide, of whom 236,000 died — down from 433,000 deaths in 1990.
wowFactor: There is a region in Africa so consistently hit by meningitis outbreaks every single year that it has its own name: the meningitis belt.

statement: Dementia is the seventh leading cause of death worldwide, with 10 million new cases every year — approximately one every three seconds.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, dementia is the seventh leading cause of death worldwide with 10 million new cases every year — approximately one new case every three seconds. In the UK specifically, it is the single leading cause of death.
wowFactor: By the time you read this sentence, three more people around the world have developed dementia.

statement: Many people aged 90 and above show no signs of dementia, proving it is not a normal part of aging.

wowFactor: Plenty of 90-year-olds have crystal-clear minds — dementia is not your brain's inevitable fate, no matter how old you get!

statement: Bipolar disorder was previously known as manic depression.
quizQuestion: What was bipolar disorder previously called?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, bipolar disorder (BD) was previously known as manic depression. The name change better reflects the cycling nature of the condition — alternating between depressive episodes and elevated mood states — and also reduces the stigma associated with the older term.
wowFactor: The condition once called manic depression has been renamed bipolar disorder to better describe its swinging moods — and reduce the stigma of its older name.

statement: Genetic factors may account for up to 70–90% of the risk of developing bipolar disorder.
quizQuestion: What percentage of bipolar disorder risk is attributed to genetic factors?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, while the causes of bipolar disorder are not fully understood, genetic factors may account for as much as 70–90% of the risk. Environmental risks such as childhood abuse and chronic stress also play a role, but genetics appears to be the dominant factor.
wowFactor: Bipolar disorder is up to 90% heritable — one of the most strongly genetic mental disorders known to science.

statement: Only arthropods, cephalopods, and vertebrates have a true brain — most other animals lack one entirely.
quizQuestion: Which invertebrate groups share the trait of a true brain with vertebrates?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, only three animal groups have a true brain: arthropods (insects, crustaceans, spiders), cephalopods (octopuses, squids), and vertebrates. Other animals like sponges and diploblasts lack a central nervous system entirely, though some have precursor structures.
wowFactor: Octopuses share one of nature's most exclusive clubs — only three animal groups in all of life's history evolved a true brain, and humans are one of them!

variants.question: Which invertebrate groups share the trait of a true brain with vertebrates?
True or false: sponges have a central nervous system.

statement: The commonly recognized sensory systems include vision, hearing, touch, taste, smell, balance, and visceral sensation — at least seven distinct senses.
quizQuestion: Besides the five classic senses, which two additional ones does the sensory nervous system commonly include?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the sensory nervous system commonly recognizes seven senses: vision, hearing, touch, taste, smell, balance (vestibular sense), and visceral sensation (sensing the state of internal organs). Most people learn only five, so balance and visceral sensation are often forgotten.
wowFactor: You actually have at least seven senses, not five — your body secretly senses its own internal organs and tracks your balance around the clock!

statement: Neurology is a nonsurgical specialty — its corresponding surgical specialty is neurosurgery.
quizQuestion: What is the surgical counterpart to the nonsurgical specialty of neurology?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, neurology is a nonsurgical medical specialty focused on diagnosing and treating nervous system disorders. Its corresponding surgical specialty — neurosurgery — handles cases requiring physical intervention. Neurologists treat conditions like stroke, epilepsy, Parkinson's disease, and Alzheimer's without operating.
wowFactor: Neurologists diagnose and treat brain diseases but never cut — the moment surgery is needed, that is a whole different doctor: the neurosurgeon!

statement: A heart attack (myocardial infarction) occurs when a coronary artery is blocked, cutting off blood supply to part of the heart muscle.
quizQuestion: What triggers a heart attack at the most basic level?
distractors: An electrical short in the heart
A blood clot in the brain
explanation: According to Wikipedia, a myocardial infarction (heart attack) occurs when a coronary artery becomes blocked, interrupting blood supply to the heart muscle (myocardium). The classic symptom is retrosternal chest pain radiating to the left shoulder, arm, or jaw. In 2015 there were 15.9 million heart attacks worldwide.
wowFactor: Heart attack pain classically radiates to the left arm and jaw — your brain gets confused about where the pain is actually coming from because nerves share the same pathways.

statement: Normal blood pressure is approximately 120/80 mmHg, and high blood pressure affects about one third of the global population.
quizQuestion: What is the normal resting blood pressure reading in millimetres of mercury?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, normal blood pressure is approximately 120/80 mmHg. Arterial hypertension (high blood pressure) is defined as a systolic pressure consistently above 130 or diastolic above 80. It affects about 33% of the global population and was responsible for approximately 19% of all deaths in 2019.
wowFactor: High blood pressure — often with zero symptoms — is responsible for nearly one in five deaths globally. The silent killer earned its nickname.

statement: At any given moment, approximately 70% of the body's total blood volume is held in the veins.
quizQuestion: What percentage of the body's blood is in the veins at any given time?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, veins serve as the primary blood reservoir in the body — at any moment approximately 70% of total blood volume resides in the venous system. This is why veins are sometimes called capacitance vessels. They carry deoxygenated blood back toward the heart and lungs.
wowFactor: Your veins are basically a giant blood storage tank — 70% of all the blood in your body is sitting in them right now.

variants.question: What percentage of the body's blood is in the veins at any given time?

statement: The spleen filters blood, stores monocytes, and recycles iron from dead red blood cells.
quizQuestion: Which organ recycles iron from worn-out red blood cells?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the spleen performs several critical functions: filtering blood to remove old or damaged red blood cells, storing monocytes (a type of white blood cell), and recycling the iron contained in red blood cell hemoglobin. Despite all this, humans can survive without a spleen, though they become more susceptible to infections.
wowFactor: Your spleen is your body's secret iron recycling plant — it strips iron out of old red blood cells to be reused in new ones.

statement: In 2024, 828 million people worldwide had diabetes — up from 200 million in 1990, a more than fourfold increase.
quizQuestion: How many people worldwide had diabetes as of 2024?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, diabetes mellitus affected 828 million people in 2024 compared to 200 million in 1990 — a greater than fourfold increase in just 34 years. Type 2 diabetes accounts for approximately 95% of all cases and costs the global economy around $760 billion per year.
wowFactor: Diabetes has quadrupled since 1990 — 828 million people now have it worldwide, and type 2 makes up 95% of all cases.

statement: Anemia is the most common blood disorder in the world, and iron-deficiency anemia alone affects approximately 1 billion people.
quizQuestion: How many people worldwide are affected by iron-deficiency anemia?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, anemia is the world's most common blood disorder, affecting between one-fifth and one-third of the global population. Iron-deficiency anemia — the most prevalent form — affects approximately 1 billion people. It occurs when hemoglobin levels fall too low to carry enough oxygen to the body's tissues.
wowFactor: Anemia is the world's most common blood disorder — and its most frequent cause, iron deficiency, affects 1 in 7 people alive right now.

statement: White blood cells make up only about 1% of blood volume, yet they are the body's entire immune defense force.
quizQuestion: What percentage of total blood volume do white blood cells make up?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, white blood cells (leukocytes) are produced in the bone marrow and make up only about 1% of total blood volume — approximately 4 to 11 billion cells per litre of blood. Despite their small proportion, they are the body's entire immune system, defending against bacteria, viruses, parasites, and cancerous cells.
wowFactor: Your entire immune army — every pathogen-killing, virus-fighting white blood cell — makes up just 1% of your blood. The rest is just transport.

statement: The vertebrate circulatory system is closed and has two divisions: the systemic circulation (body) and the pulmonary circulation (lungs).
quizQuestion: What are the two major divisions of the vertebrate circulatory system?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the vertebrate circulatory system is a closed system with two major divisions: systemic circulation (carrying oxygenated blood from the heart throughout the body) and pulmonary circulation (carrying deoxygenated blood to the lungs to be re-oxygenated). The heart acts as a dual pump driving both circuits.
wowFactor: Your heart is actually two pumps in one — the left side drives blood around the whole body, while the right side simultaneously pumps it to the lungs and back.

statement: When stressed, the body activates two major systems: the sympathetic nervous system triggers fight-or-flight, while the HPA axis releases cortisol.
quizQuestion: Which hormone does the HPA axis release during stress?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the HPA axis regulates the release of cortisol, which influences metabolic, psychological, and immunological functions. The SAM axis separately triggers adrenaline as part of the immediate fight-or-flight response — two distinct systems firing at once.
wowFactor: Stress fires two separate alarm systems simultaneously — one fast (adrenaline) and one slow (cortisol) — each wiring the body for danger in completely different ways.

statement: The stress response is regulated by several brain regions including the amygdala, prefrontal cortex, hypothalamus, and limbic system working in concert.
quizQuestion: Which brain structure is the emotional threat detector that triggers stress responses?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the SAM and HPA stress axes are regulated by several brain regions including the limbic system, prefrontal cortex, amygdala, hypothalamus, and stria terminalis. Through these mechanisms, stress can alter memory, reward, immune function, metabolism, and disease susceptibility.
wowFactor: Your amygdala is a tiny almond-shaped alarm system buried deep in your brain — it makes your heart pound before you've even consciously registered what scared you.

statement: Humans produce two well-known hormones during stressful situations: adrenaline (epinephrine) and cortisol.
quizQuestion: Name one of the two well-known hormones humans produce during stressful situations.
explanation: According to Wikipedia, two well-known hormones that humans produce during stressful situations are adrenaline and cortisol. Adrenaline provides the immediate surge (SAM axis) while cortisol has longer-lasting metabolic, psychological, and immunological effects (HPA axis).
wowFactor: Stress hormones come in fast and slow varieties — adrenaline hits in seconds, but cortisol lingers for hours, quietly reshaping your immune system and metabolism long after the danger has passed.

statement: ADHD is primarily genetic, with meta-analyses showing a heritability rate of 70-80% — as heritable as height.
quizQuestion: What is the approximate heritability rate of ADHD according to meta-analyses?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, meta-analyses have shown ADHD is primarily genetic with a heritability rate of 70-80%, with risk factors being highly accumulative. Environmental risks are not related to social or familial factors — they act very early in prenatal or early postnatal life.
wowFactor: ADHD is about as heritable as height — around 75% genetic — meaning if a parent has it, the odds their child does too are far higher than most people realize.

statement: People with ADHD can paradoxically enter 'hyperfocus' — intense locked-in concentration on rewarding tasks — despite general difficulty sustaining attention.
quizQuestion: What is the paradoxical state of intense locked-in focus sometimes seen in ADHD called?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, ADHD's inhibitory deficits can lead to difficulty stopping an already ongoing response pattern — manifesting as hyperfocus, where someone continues an action despite intending to stop. This symptom is related to addiction risks and is colloquially well-known.
wowFactor: ADHD isn't simply an inability to focus — the same brain that can't sit through a lecture can become impossibly locked onto a video game for hours. Hyperfocus is real and it's wired into the disorder.

statement: ADHD symptoms arise from executive dysfunction — impairing time management, cognitive inhibition, task initiation, and sustained attention.
quizQuestion: ADHD symptoms arise from deficits in what type of brain function?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, ADHD symptoms arise from executive dysfunction — specifically deficits in self-regulation such as time management, cognitive inhibition, task initiation, and sustained attention. These lead to poor professional performance, relationship difficulties, and numerous health risks.
wowFactor: ADHD's struggles with time management and starting tasks aren't laziness or bad character — they're a measurable deficit in executive function, the brain's management system.

statement: The cerebellar cortex appears to have fine parallel grooves, but it's actually a single thin continuous layer of tissue tightly folded like an accordion.
quizQuestion: How is the cerebellar cortex arranged beneath its parallel surface grooves?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the parallel grooves of the cerebellum conceal the fact that the cerebellar cortex is actually a single thin continuous layer tightly folded accordion-style. Within this layer, the most important neurons are Purkinje cells and granule cells, giving rise to massive signal-processing capability.
wowFactor: The cerebellum looks like dozens of tiny ridges — but it's actually ONE continuous sheet of tissue folded like an accordion. Unrolled, it would be enormous.

statement: Each cerebellar Purkinje cell receives a powerful 'teaching signal' from a single climbing fiber, which induces lasting changes in how thousands of weaker inputs are processed.
quizQuestion: What role does a climbing fiber play for each Purkinje cell in the cerebellum?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Marr-Albus theory proposes that each Purkinje cell receives thousands of weak inputs from granule cells via parallel fibers, plus one extremely strong input from a single climbing fiber acting as a 'teaching signal' — inducing long-lasting changes in parallel fiber strength, forming the basis of cerebellar motor learning.
wowFactor: Your brain has a built-in 'teacher' — one climbing fiber sends a powerful signal to each Purkinje cell, literally rewiring how thousands of other inputs are weighted, so your movements keep improving.

variants.question: True or false: the cerebellum is necessary for motor learning, not just movement execution.

visualDescription: A glowing Purkinje cell illustrated like a pixel-art tree, one blazing fiber descending from above like lightning — the climbing fiber teacher — while hundreds of tiny threads feed in from the sides.

statement: Although the cerebellum is usually smaller than the cerebrum, in mormyrid fish it can be as large as or even larger than the cerebrum.
quizQuestion: In which animal can the cerebellum be as large as or larger than the cerebrum?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, although the cerebellum is usually smaller than the cerebrum in most animals, in some animals such as the mormyrid fishes it may be as large as or even larger than the cerebrum — a striking anatomical exception to the usual brain organization.
wowFactor: In most animals the cerebellum is tiny compared to the cerebrum — but in the mormyrid fish, the cerebellum is so oversized it can dwarf the rest of the brain, making it a bizarre outlier in vertebrate neuroscience.

statement: The word 'coma' comes from Ancient Greek 'koma', meaning 'deep sleep' — a fitting name for a state of profound unconsciousness.
quizQuestion: What does the Ancient Greek word 'koma', origin of 'coma', mean?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the word coma derives from Ancient Greek 'koma' meaning 'deep sleep'. A true coma involves complete absence of wakefulness — the person cannot be awakened, fails to respond to stimuli, and lacks a normal sleep-wake cycle.
wowFactor: The ancient Greeks named coma 'deep sleep' centuries before medicine understood that true coma involves no sleep at all — just a complete absence of consciousness.

statement: Consciousness requires two distinct components: wakefulness (degree of alertness, from the brainstem) and awareness (qualitative cognition, from the cerebral cortex).
quizQuestion: What are the two components both required to maintain consciousness?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, for a patient to maintain consciousness, both wakefulness (quantitative assessment of alertness degree) and awareness (qualitative assessment of cerebral cortex functions like attention, memory, language, and spatial orientation) must be maintained.
wowFactor: Being 'conscious' requires two completely separate systems working together — wakefulness from the brainstem and awareness from the cortex. Lose either one and consciousness vanishes.

statement: Symptoms of trauma-related mental disorders have been documented since at least ancient Greek times — thousands of years before PTSD was formally named.
quizQuestion: How far back have symptoms of trauma-related mental disorders been documented?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, symptoms of trauma-related mental disorders have been documented since at least ancient Greek times. Samuel Pepys's 1666 diary described intrusive symptoms after the Great Fire of London, and the condition was known under various names during the world wars before PTSD was formally recognized.
wowFactor: PTSD is not a modern invention — ancient Greek soldiers described its symptoms thousands of years ago. Humanity named the Great Fire of London's trauma in a diary centuries before medicine recognized the disorder.

variants.question: True or false: PTSD is more common in women than in men.
In the US, approximately what percentage of adults experience PTSD in a given year?
Samuel Pepys described intrusive distressing symptoms after witnessing which 1666 disaster?
PTSD symptoms must last more than ___ after the traumatic event to qualify for diagnosis.

sensitivityNote: PTSD involves trauma and mental illness; framed historically and epidemiologically. No graphic descriptions of traumatic events.
visualDescription: A split pixel-art panel: left shows an ancient Greek soldier with a haunted expression; right shows a modern figure with the same expression — connected by a glowing thread labeled 'PTSD'.

statement: People who experience interpersonal violence such as rape or physical abuse are more likely to develop PTSD than those who experience non-assault traumas like accidents or natural disasters.
quizQuestion: Which type of trauma makes PTSD development more likely than accidents or natural disasters?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, people who experience interpersonal violence such as rape, other sexual assaults, being kidnapped, stalking, physical abuse by an intimate partner, and childhood abuse are more likely to develop PTSD than those who experience non-assault based trauma such as accidents and natural disasters.
wowFactor: It's not just the severity of trauma that determines PTSD risk — it's the type. Interpersonal violence from another person carries far higher PTSD risk than equally terrifying impersonal events like natural disasters.

variants.question: True or false: most people who experience traumatic events develop PTSD.
In the US, roughly what percentage of people develop PTSD at some point in their lives?
In conflict zones and war-exposed regions, PTSD rates are significantly ___ than in non-conflict countries.
Which mental disorder has counselling (psychotherapy) as its main recommended treatment?

sensitivityNote: References sexual assault and domestic violence as PTSD causes; framing is clinical and epidemiological. Age-gated adult due to topic sensitivity.
visualDescription: A scale in pixel art: one side holds a natural disaster (storm, earthquake) while the other holds a shadowed figure representing interpersonal threat — the interpersonal side tipping lower, heavier.

statement: The term 'neurosis' was officially removed from both the WHO's ICD and the APA's DSM, and the American Heritage Medical Dictionary declared it 'no longer used in psychiatric diagnosis' in 2007.
quizQuestion: Which two diagnostic systems officially removed 'neurosis' from psychiatric use?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the term 'neurosis' is no longer used in disorder names by the WHO's International Classification of Diseases (ICD) or the American Psychiatric Association's Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM). The American Heritage Medical Dictionary of 2007 confirmed it is 'no longer used in psychiatric diagnosis'.
wowFactor: Neurosis — once a major psychiatric label applied to all anxiety disorders — was quietly retired from official medicine by both the WHO and APA. Yet people still say 'I'm being neurotic' every day without knowing it's been officially abolished.

variants.question: True or false: 'neurosis' is still an official diagnostic category in the current DSM.
What personality trait concept descends from the abandoned psychiatric label 'neurosis'?
Neurosis is distinct from psychosis because neurosis does NOT involve what?
Neuroticism is now recognized as one of the ___ personality traits.

sensitivityNote: Discusses psychiatric terminology history; neutral and educational framing.
visualDescription: A pixel-art medical textbook with the word 'NEUROSIS' crossed out in red, replaced by a scattered cluster of modern diagnostic labels — representing psychiatry's evolution.

statement: Neurosis is distinguished from psychosis in that neurosis does NOT involve a loss of touch with reality — the person remains aware of their surroundings.
quizQuestion: What key symptom does neurosis lack that distinguishes it from psychosis?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, neurosis is distinguished from psychosis, which refers to a loss of touch with reality. Its descendant concept, neuroticism, refers to a personality trait of being prone to anxiousness and mental collapse — now one of the recognized Big Five personality traits.
wowFactor: The key difference between 'neurotic' and 'psychotic' isn't severity — it's reality. Someone neurotic is anxious about the real world; someone psychotic has lost their grip on what the real world is.

variants.question: True or false: neuroticism is currently used as a formal psychiatric diagnosis.
Which Big Five personality trait evolved from the concept of neurosis?
A patient is intensely anxious and distressed but fully aware of where they are and what is real. Is this neurosis or psychosis?
The ICD-11 includes a similar concept to neurosis under the condition called 'negative ___'.

sensitivityNote: Distinguishes psychiatric concepts; neutral educational framing.
visualDescription: Two pixel-art figures: one labeled 'Neurosis' standing anxiously but grounded in a real landscape; one labeled 'Psychosis' floating in a fractured surreal world — illustrating the reality distinction.

statement: In 2015, encephalitis was estimated to have affected 4.3 million people and caused 150,000 deaths worldwide.
quizQuestion: How many deaths did encephalitis cause worldwide in 2015?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 2015 encephalitis was estimated to have affected 4.3 million people and resulted in 150,000 deaths worldwide. Causes include viruses like herpes simplex and rabies, bacteria, fungi, parasites, and autoimmune diseases.
wowFactor: Brain inflammation from viruses and autoimmune reactions kills 150,000 people a year globally — yet most people have never heard of encephalitis until someone they know gets it.

variants.question: How many people worldwide were estimated to have encephalitis in 2015?
True or false: certain types of encephalitis are preventable with vaccines.
A patient presents with brain inflammation, fever, confusion, and a stiff neck. What condition is most likely?
Encephalitis can be caused by viruses, bacteria, fungi, parasites, or ___ diseases.

sensitivityNote: Medical condition; framing is epidemiological and educational.
visualDescription: A pixel-art brain rendered with an angry red glow, with floating charts showing 4.3M affected and 150K deaths — the hidden scale of encephalitis visualized.

statement: Encephalitis can be triggered by a person's own immune system attacking the brain — autoimmune encephalitis — not just by infections from outside the body.
quizQuestion: Besides infections, what unexpected internal source can cause encephalitis?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, encephalitis causes include viruses like herpes simplex and rabies, bacteria, fungi, parasites, autoimmune diseases, and certain medications. In many cases the cause remains unknown. The body's own immune system attacking the brain is a recognized distinct cause.
wowFactor: The immune system is supposed to be your defender — but autoimmune encephalitis shows it can become one of the most dangerous threats to your own brain.

variants.question: True or false: the cause of encephalitis is always identified in every diagnosed case.
What antiviral drug is commonly used to treat herpes simplex encephalitis?
Brain inflammation presenting with fever, confusion, aphasia, and possible seizures describes which condition?
Severe encephalitis patients may require ___ if unable to breathe independently.

sensitivityNote: Medical condition; framing is educational and neutral.
visualDescription: A pixel-art immune cell in warrior armor accidentally charging toward a glowing brain — representing autoimmune encephalitis, where the body's defender attacks the brain.

statement: The WHO defines mental health as a state of well-being where a person realizes their abilities, copes with normal life stresses, works productively, and contributes to their community.
quizQuestion: According to the WHO, mental health is a state of well-being — not merely the absence of what?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the WHO defines mental health as a 'state of well-being' where the individual realizes their abilities, can cope with normal life stresses, work productively, and contribute to their community. From a holistic perspective, mental health is not merely the absence of mental illness.
wowFactor: The WHO says mental health isn't just 'not being sick' — it's an active state of thriving and contributing. Most people confuse the two, treating 'no diagnosis' as the definition of mental health.

variants.question: True or false: according to positive psychology, mental health is merely the absence of mental illness.
Mental health encompasses emotional, psychological, and what third dimension of well-being?
Mental health affects how people manage stress, interpersonal relationships, and ___.
A person with no diagnosis who thrives at work, maintains relationships, and handles adversity well — do they exemplify good mental health per the WHO?
distractors: Only if verified by a psychiatrist

sensitivityNote: Mental health is a sensitive topic; framing is empowering and destigmatizing per WHO language.
visualDescription: A pixel-art human figure standing tall in sunlight, with interconnected rings labeled emotional, psychological, and social floating around them — representing the WHO model of mental health.

statement: The cerebrum controls all voluntary actions in the human body, working in concert with the cerebellum.
quizQuestion: Which brain structure controls all voluntary human actions?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the cerebrum — also called the telencephalon or endbrain — works with the cerebellum to control all voluntary actions in the human body. It includes the cerebral cortex, hippocampus, basal ganglia, and olfactory bulb.
wowFactor: Every intentional movement you make — picking up a cup, typing, dancing — is orchestrated by the cerebrum, the brain's massive command center.

variants.question: Which structure contains the hippocampus, basal ganglia, and olfactory bulb?
True or false: the cerebrum is also known as the telencephalon.
The cerebrum controls all ___ actions in the human body.
Which part of the forebrain develops into the cerebral cortex in mammals?

visualDescription: A glowing cross-section of a human brain with the massive cerebrum lit up in gold, its two hemispheres towering above a tiny cerebellum below, neural pathways sparkling like city lights.

statement: The cerebrum is divided into approximately symmetric left and right cerebral hemispheres.
quizQuestion: Into how many hemispheres is the cerebrum divided?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the cerebrum is divided into approximately symmetric left and right cerebral hemispheres. In mammals, the outer pallium (dorsal telencephalon) develops into the cerebral cortex, while the inner subpallium becomes the basal ganglia.
wowFactor: Your cerebrum is a two-part organ — and the two halves, though nearly mirror images, handle surprisingly different jobs like language versus spatial reasoning.

variants.question: True or false: the left and right cerebral hemispheres are perfectly symmetric.
The ventral telencephalon of the cerebrum develops into the ___.
What structure connects the two cerebral hemispheres beneath the cortex?
The hippocampus, basal ganglia, and olfactory bulb are all found within which brain region?

visualDescription: A top-down view of a glowing human brain split cleanly down the middle into two near-mirror hemispheres, each pulsing with soft blue and gold light.

statement: Secondary (metastatic) brain tumors are about four times more common than primary brain tumors.
quizQuestion: How much more common are secondary brain tumors than primary ones?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, secondary (metastatic) brain tumors — cancers that spread to the brain from elsewhere in the body — are about four times as common as primary brain tumors that originate within the brain. About half of all brain metastases come from lung cancer.
wowFactor: Most brain tumors didn't actually start in the brain — they hitchhiked there from somewhere else, with lung cancer being the most common stowaway.

variants.question: Which organ is the most common original source of brain metastases?
True or false: primary brain tumors are more common than metastatic ones.
Brain tumors that spread from outside the brain are called ___ tumors.
A brain tumor originated from a distant organ. What type is it most likely?

sensitivityNote: Discusses cancer; age-appropriate for teens and adults
visualDescription: A dark pixel-art cross-section of a human skull showing two tumor masses glowing red — one small primary tumor deep within and four larger secondary tumors scattered around it.

statement: The average five-year survival rate for all malignant brain cancers in the United States is 33%.
quizQuestion: What is the average US five-year survival rate for malignant brain cancers?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the average five-year survival rate for all malignant brain cancers in the United States is 33%. Outcomes vary significantly by tumor type — malignant glioblastomas have very poor outcomes while benign meningiomas typically have good outcomes.
wowFactor: Brain cancer survival varies wildly by type — benign meningiomas are usually curable, while glioblastomas remain one of medicine's most stubborn challenges.

variants.question: True or false: benign brain tumors can still be life-threatening.
Which brain tumor type typically has the worst survival outcomes?
The most common primary brain tumor in adults is ___, which is usually benign.
In children under 15, brain tumors are second only to which cancer?

sensitivityNote: Cancer survival statistics; adult content due to medical gravity
visualDescription: A hospital corridor in pixel art, a doctor holding up a brain scan glowing blue, the number 33% etched in the wall as a stark reminder of survival odds.

statement: Up to 4% of brain cancers may be caused by CT scan radiation.
quizQuestion: What common medical scan may cause up to 4% of brain cancers?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, while the cause of most brain tumors is unknown, up to 4% of brain cancers may be linked to CT scan radiation. Other uncommon risk factors include vinyl chloride exposure, Epstein-Barr virus, ionizing radiation, and inherited syndromes like neurofibromatosis.
wowFactor: A scan designed to detect cancer might itself cause it — CT scans are invaluable tools but carry a small, real radiation risk for brain cancer.

variants.question: True or false: mobile phone exposure has been shown to clearly increase brain tumor risk.
The most common primary brain tumor in children is malignant ___.
Which virus is listed as an uncommon risk factor for brain cancer?
Which inherited syndrome is a known risk factor for brain tumors?

sensitivityNote: Medical risk factors for cancer

statement: 75 to 90 percent of people with intellectual disability have the mild form.
quizQuestion: What percentage of people with intellectual disability have the mild form?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, intellectual disability affects about 2-3% of the general population, and 75 to 90% of those affected have mild intellectual disability. Non-syndromic (idiopathic) cases account for 30-50% of all cases.
wowFactor: Most people with intellectual disability have the mild form — meaning the vast majority live relatively independent lives, far from the severe picture many people imagine.

variants.question: True or false: about a quarter of intellectual disability cases are caused by a genetic disorder.
Down syndrome and fragile X syndrome are examples of which type of intellectual disability?
Intellectual disability is characterized by IQ below ___ and deficits in adaptive behaviors.

sensitivityNote: Discusses disability; handled respectfully with clinical framing

statement: Intellectual disability affects about 2-3% of the general population worldwide.
quizQuestion: Roughly what percentage of the global population has intellectual disability?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, intellectual disability affects about 2-3% of the general population. About 5% of cases are inherited, and cases of unknown cause affected about 95 million people as of 2013.
wowFactor: With 2-3% prevalence, intellectual disability affects hundreds of millions of people worldwide — making it one of the most common neurodevelopmental conditions.

variants.question: True or false: about 5% of intellectual disability cases are inherited.
Intellectual disability is also known as general learning disability in the ___.
According to DSM-5, intellectual functions include reasoning, planning, and learning from ___.

sensitivityNote: Discusses disability prevalence; clinical and factual

statement: Unlike the central nervous system, the peripheral nervous system has no blood-brain barrier protection, leaving it exposed to toxins.
quizQuestion: Which nervous system division lacks blood-brain barrier protection?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, unlike the CNS (protected by the vertebral column, skull, and blood-brain barrier), the peripheral nervous system is exposed to toxins because it lacks these protections. This makes peripheral nerves vulnerable to poisons, infections, and metabolic damage.
wowFactor: Your brain and spinal cord are locked in a fortress — but the nerves running through your limbs and organs are completely unshielded from toxins in your blood.

variants.question: True or false: the peripheral nervous system is protected by the blood-brain barrier.
Which nervous system division connects the brain and spinal cord to the limbs and organs?
The PNS can be divided into a somatic division and an ___ division.

visualDescription: A pixel-art human silhouette: the brain and spine glow behind fortress walls, but the nerves branching out to the arms and legs are exposed, unshielded, pulsing with faint orange light.

statement: The autonomic division of the peripheral nervous system exerts involuntary control over smooth muscle and glands.
quizQuestion: Which PNS division controls smooth muscle and glands involuntarily?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the autonomic division of the peripheral nervous system exerts involuntary control over smooth muscle and glands, regulating functions like heart rate, digestion, and glandular secretion without conscious effort.
wowFactor: Right now, without any conscious thought, your autonomic nervous system is adjusting your heart rate, dilating blood vessels, and regulating digestion — all automatically.

variants.question: True or false: the autonomic nervous system controls voluntary muscle movements.
Smooth muscle and glands are controlled by which division of the PNS?
The peripheral nervous system connects the CNS to the ___ and organs.

statement: The human brain contains more than 86 billion neurons, plus roughly an equal number of glial cells.
quizQuestion: How many neurons are in the human brain?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, there are more than 86 billion neurons in the human brain, plus a roughly equal number of non-neuronal (glial) cells. Brain activity is made possible by interconnections between neurons and their release of neurotransmitters in response to nerve impulses.
wowFactor: 86 billion neurons — that's more than 10 times the number of people who have ever lived on Earth, all packed inside your skull firing in concert.

variants.question: True or false: there are approximately as many glial cells as neurons in the human brain.
Which organ contains more than 86 billion interconnected neurons?

statement: The human brain is isolated from the bloodstream by the blood-brain barrier.
quizQuestion: What barrier isolates the human brain from the bloodstream?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the human brain is protected by the skull, suspended in cerebrospinal fluid, and isolated from the bloodstream by the blood-brain barrier. This barrier prevents many pathogens and toxins from entering brain tissue — but also makes treating brain diseases with drugs notoriously difficult.
wowFactor: The blood-brain barrier that keeps your brain safe from toxins is the same reason treating brain diseases with drugs is so incredibly hard — most medicines simply cannot get through.

variants.question: True or false: the blood-brain barrier makes it harder to treat brain diseases with drugs.

The human brain consists of the cerebrum, the brainstem, and the ___.
The largest commissural nerve tract connecting the two brain hemispheres is the ___.

visualDescription: A pixel-art cross-section of a human head showing red blood vessels stopped at an invisible glowing wall just before reaching the brain — the blood-brain barrier as an electric force field.

statement: Each cerebral hemisphere is divided into four lobes — frontal, parietal, temporal, and occipital — and the occipital lobe is dedicated to vision.
quizQuestion: Which brain lobe is dedicated entirely to processing vision?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the occipital lobe of the cerebrum is dedicated to vision. The frontal lobe handles executive functions including self-control, planning, reasoning, and abstract thought. Each hemisphere has four major lobes in total.
wowFactor: The entire back portion of your brain — the occipital lobe — exists solely to process what your eyes see. Vision is so demanding it gets its own dedicated lobe.

variants.question: Self-control, planning, and abstract thinking are primarily handled by which brain lobe?

visualDescription: A pixel-art brain viewed from the side, each of the four lobes colored differently — blue frontal, green parietal, yellow temporal, red occipital — each glowing with its specialized function.

statement: Every neuron in nervous tissue has an axon — the long stem-like part that sends action potentials to the next cell.
quizQuestion: What part of a neuron sends action potentials to the next cell?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, all neurons in nervous tissue have an axon — the long stem-like projection that transmits action potentials to the next cell. Bundles of axons make up nerves in the peripheral nervous system and tracts in the central nervous system.
wowFactor: Every neuron, no matter where it is in your body, has exactly one axon — a biological wire that fires electrical signals to pass messages down the line.

variants.question: True or false: bundles of axons make up nerves in the peripheral nervous system.

statement: Nervous tissue performs five key functions: sensory input, integration, muscle and gland control, homeostasis, and mental activity.
quizQuestion: How many key functions does nervous tissue perform?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the functions of nervous tissue include sensory input, integration, control of muscles and glands, homeostasis, and mental activity — covering everything from reflexes to consciousness.
wowFactor: Nervous tissue doesn't just carry signals — it is responsible for everything from breathing to dreaming, all five functions woven into every thought and heartbeat.

variants.question: True or false: homeostasis is one of the five key functions of nervous tissue.

statement: The brainstem makes up only about 2.6% of the brain's total weight, yet controls heart rate and breathing.
quizQuestion: What percentage of the brain's total weight is the brainstem?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the brainstem is very small, making up around only 2.6% of the brain's total weight. Despite its size, it has critical roles in regulating heart rate and respiratory function, and provides the main motor and sensory nerve supply to the face and neck via cranial nerves.
wowFactor: The tiny brainstem — just 2.6% of your brain's weight — is so critical that damage to it is instantly fatal. It controls your heartbeat and breathing on autopilot.

visualDescription: A pixel-art brain with the tiny glowing brainstem highlighted at its base — pulsing red with a heartbeat rhythm — the smallest piece controlling the most vital functions.

statement: Ten pairs of cranial nerves originate from the brainstem, providing the main motor and sensory supply to the face and neck.
quizQuestion: How many pairs of cranial nerves originate from the brainstem?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the brainstem provides the main motor and sensory nerve supply to the face and neck via cranial nerves, with ten pairs of cranial nerves coming from it. The brainstem also plays a prime role in conveying motor and sensory pathways between the brain and body.
wowFactor: The tiny brainstem is the Grand Central of your cranial nerve network — 10 of the 12 cranial nerves that control your face, hearing, taste, and swallowing all plug directly into it.

variants.question: True or false: the brainstem also regulates the body's sleep cycle.

visualDescription: A pixel-art brainstem glowing at the base of the brain, ten bright nerve cables fanning out like wires toward the face and neck — the body's communication hub.

statement: The six-layered neocortex makes up approximately 90% of the cerebral cortex.
quizQuestion: What percentage of the cerebral cortex is the six-layered neocortex?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the six-layered neocortex makes up approximately 90% of the cortex, with the allocortex (which has only three or four layers) making up the remainder. The cortex plays a key role in attention, perception, awareness, thought, memory, language, and consciousness.
wowFactor: Ninety percent of your outer brain is built in six precise layers — like a six-story biological circuit board responsible for everything you think, feel, and remember.

variants.question: True or false: the neocortex has six neuronal layers, while the allocortex has three or four.

statement: In most large-brained mammals, the cerebral cortex is folded to provide greater surface area inside the skull; small-brained mammals have smooth cortices.
quizQuestion: Why is the cerebral cortex folded in large-brained mammals?
acceptableAnswers: more surface area in less space
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in most mammals with large brains, the cerebral cortex is folded, providing a greater surface area in the confined volume of the cranium. Mammals with small brains have smooth cortices. The folds are called gyri and the grooves are called sulci.
wowFactor: Your brain is so big it had to crumple itself to fit inside your skull — if you unfolded the human cerebral cortex, it would spread out to about the size of a pillowcase.

variants.question: True or false: small-brained mammals have smooth, unfolded cerebral cortices.

statement: Ganglia are clusters of neuron cell bodies located in the peripheral nervous system, acting as relay stations outside the brain and spinal cord.
quizQuestion: Where in the body are ganglia — clusters of neuron cell bodies — located?
acceptableAnswers: outside the brain and spinal cord
explanation: According to Wikipedia, ganglia are groups of neuron cell bodies in the peripheral nervous system — outside the central nervous system. They serve as relay hubs for both the somatic and autonomic nervous systems, including sympathetic and parasympathetic branches.
wowFactor: Your body has mini-brains scattered outside your skull and spine — ganglia act as local relay stations that can process signals without involving the brain at all!

variants.question: What is the name for a cluster of neuron cell bodies in the peripheral nervous system?

statement: The autonomic nervous system has both sympathetic and parasympathetic ganglia that contain the cell bodies of postganglionic neurons controlling involuntary functions.
quizQuestion: Which nervous system division contains both sympathetic and parasympathetic ganglia?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the autonomic nervous system contains both sympathetic and parasympathetic ganglia, which house the cell bodies of postganglionic neurons. These control involuntary functions like heart rate, digestion, and glandular activity.
wowFactor: Your heart rate, digestion, and sweat glands are controlled by tiny nerve clusters — ganglia — that operate completely without your conscious awareness.

variants.question: True or false: ganglia in the somatic nervous system include dorsal root ganglia.

statement: The somatosensory cortex contains a map of the body called a sensory homunculus, where neighboring neurons represent nearby body locations.
quizQuestion: What is the brain's body map in the somatosensory cortex called?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the somatosensory cortex preserves the spatial layout of the body — neighboring neurons represent nearby skin or body locations, creating what is called a sensory homunculus. This map is wildly distorted: the hands and lips take up far more space than the back.
wowFactor: Your brain has a warped little body-map inside it — the sensory homunculus — where your lips and hands are gigantic but your entire torso is tiny!

variants.question: True or false: the somatosensory cortex maps the body so nearby neurons represent nearby body locations.

statement: The somatosensory system handles not just touch, but also proprioception — the sense of body position and balance.
quizQuestion: Which sense — body position and balance — is part of the somatosensory system?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the somatosensory system's main functions include perception of external stimuli, perception of internal stimuli, AND regulation of body position and balance — a sense known as proprioception. This is why you can touch your nose with your eyes closed.
wowFactor: Close your eyes and touch your nose — that's proprioception at work, a hidden sixth sense built right into your somatosensory system!

variants.question: True or false: the somatosensory system regulates body position and balance.
The somatosensory system includes proprioception, which is the sense of body ___ and balance.

statement: The somatosensory system has two main subdivisions: one for mechanosensory information like touch and vibration, and one for detecting pain and temperature (nociception).
quizQuestion: What are the two main subdivisions of the somatosensory system?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the somatosensory system has two subdivisions: one detects mechanosensory information (touch, vibration, pressure, tension) and the other handles nociception — detection of pain and temperature that may be harmful. These use different receptor types and pathways.
wowFactor: Your sense of touch and your sense of pain are actually two completely separate systems that just happen to live in the same somatosensory neighborhood!

variants.question: True or false: nociceptors are specialized receptors for pain signals in the somatosensory system.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, because of neuroplasticity, the brain can partially recover function by forming new neural connections to compensate for damaged areas. Patients may regain adaptive skills such as movement and speech, especially with therapy and practice.
wowFactor: The brain can literally rewire itself after damage — a process called neuroplasticity allows healthy neurons to form new connections and take over jobs that injured regions can no longer do!

variants.question: True or false: neuroplasticity allows the brain to form new connections to recover from damage.
Which brain property makes it possible to relearn speech after a stroke?

The brain forms new neural connections after injury through a process called ___.

visualDescription: A pixel art brain with a dark damaged region on one side, while glowing golden pathways arc around it — new neural bridges lighting up like rewiring a circuit board.

statement: Brain injuries have two phases: primary injuries at the moment of trauma, and secondary injuries that develop afterward due to the body's own physiological responses.
quizQuestion: What causes 'secondary' brain injuries — the phase that develops after initial trauma?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, brain injuries can be classified by timing: primary injuries occur at the moment of trauma, while secondary injuries develop afterward due to physiological responses. This means the body's own reaction to the initial injury can cause further damage.
wowFactor: After a head injury, your own body can make things worse — secondary brain injuries are caused by the body's internal physiological responses to the original trauma, not by additional blows!

variants.question: True or false: secondary brain injuries occur at the exact moment of trauma.
Which phase of brain injury is caused by the body's physiological response rather than direct trauma?
Primary brain injuries occur at the moment of trauma, while secondary injuries develop ___ due to physiological responses.
A patient with no new blows to the head shows worsening brain damage hours after injury. This is likely what?

sensitivityNote: Discusses brain injury mechanism — appropriate educational content
visualDescription: A pixel art timeline showing a head impact at moment zero, then a second wave of damage spreading hours later from within — like a fire that ignites a second fire from its own heat.

statement: As of 2015, an estimated 5% of people worldwide aged 15–65 — between 158 million and 351 million people — had used a controlled drug at least once.
quizQuestion: As of 2015, what percentage of 15–65-year-olds worldwide had used a controlled drug at least once?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, as of 2015, it is estimated that about 5% of people worldwide aged 15 to 65 (158 million to 351 million people) had used controlled drugs at least once. The wide range reflects different estimation methodologies.
wowFactor: Roughly 1 in 20 working-age adults worldwide has tried a controlled drug — that's up to 351 million people, according to 2015 estimates. That's more people than the entire US population.

variants.question: True or false: as of 2015, over 100 million people worldwide had used a controlled drug at least once.

statement: For centuries, doctors deliberately drained patients' blood as a treatment, based on the theory of four humors — a practice now considered harmful.
quizQuestion: What ancient theory justified the medical practice of bloodletting?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, ancient physicians would apply bloodletting according to the theories of humorism, or the four humors. For centuries this was mainstream medicine — now considered harmful pseudoscience, it illustrates how dramatically medical understanding can change.
wowFactor: For thousands of years, the standard treatment for almost every illness was deliberately cutting patients open to drain their blood — all based on a completely wrong theory called humorism!

variants.question: True or false: bloodletting was once considered mainstream medical treatment based on humorism.

statement: Medicine has been practiced since prehistoric times, when it was largely an art — frequently connected to religious and philosophical beliefs of local cultures.
quizQuestion: How was prehistoric medicine primarily understood before modern science?
correctAnswer: As an art tied to religion
acceptableAnswers: as an art tied to religion
explanation: According to Wikipedia, medicine has been practiced since prehistoric times, and for most of this time it was an art frequently having connections to the religious and philosophical beliefs of local culture. Only with modern science did medicine shift toward a combination of art and science.
wowFactor: For most of human history, medicine and religion were the same thing — healers were shamans mixing herbs and prayers, not scientists testing hypotheses!

variants.question: True or false: medicine was practiced since prehistoric times, long before modern science.

variants.correctAnswer: As an art with religious ties

statement: Syphilis caused approximately 107,000 deaths and affected 45.4 million people globally in 2015.
quizQuestion: Approximately how many people had syphilis globally in 2015?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 2015 syphilis affected approximately 45.4 million people worldwide, causing around 107,000 deaths. The bacterium Treponema pallidum causes the disease, which is transmitted through sexual contact and treatable with benzathine penicillin G.
wowFactor: Syphilis — a disease curable with one shot of penicillin — still infected 45 million people in 2015. Access to treatment, not medical knowledge, remains the challenge!

statement: Typhoid fever causes 12.5 million new cases per year globally, caused by the bacterium Salmonella Typhi, with vaccines that are 40-90% effective.
quizQuestion: Approximately how many new typhoid fever cases occur per year globally?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, typhoid fever is a bacterial infection caused by Salmonella Typhi that affects approximately 12.5 million people per year. Vaccines exist with 40-90% effectiveness, and the disease spreads through contaminated food and water.
wowFactor: Despite having a vaccine, typhoid fever still strikes 12.5 million people every year — mostly because contaminated water remains widespread in many parts of the world.

variants.question: True or false: typhoid fever is caused by Salmonella Typhi bacteria.

statement: About 30% of heart attack victims experience atypical symptoms — no chest pain — which are more common in women, elderly patients, and those with diabetes.
quizQuestion: Approximately what percentage of heart attack victims have no chest pain (atypical symptoms)?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, around 30% of people who have a myocardial infarction (heart attack) present with atypical symptoms such as nausea, jaw pain, or arm pain rather than classic chest pain. This is especially common in women, the elderly, and diabetic patients.
wowFactor: Nearly 1 in 3 heart attacks don't feel like a heart attack at all — silent or atypical presentations are dangerously common, especially in women!

variants.question: True or false: women are more likely than men to experience atypical heart attack symptoms.
Which groups most commonly experience atypical (no chest pain) heart attack symptoms?

statement: Cardiovascular disease caused 17.9 million deaths in 2015, accounting for 32.1% of all global deaths — the single leading cause of death worldwide.
quizQuestion: What percentage of all global deaths does cardiovascular disease cause?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, cardiovascular diseases (CVDs) are the leading cause of death globally. In 2015, CVDs caused approximately 17.9 million deaths, representing 32.1% of all deaths worldwide.
wowFactor: One in three people on Earth will die of cardiovascular disease — it kills more people than all cancers, accidents, and infectious diseases combined!

variants.question: True or false: cardiovascular disease is the single leading cause of death globally.
